questions.specification: A black metal plate and a shiny metal plate are both placed under direct sunlight. After some time, the black plate is noticeably warmer than the shiny plate.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.1 Thermal energy transfers > B.1.3 Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.3 Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Explains differential heating and cooling of black vs shiny plates via radiation: black surfaces are better absorbers/emitters, shiny surfaces reflect and are poorer emitters; part (b) also involves radiative heat loss alongside other transfer mechanisms.

parts.content: Explain why the black plate heats up more quickly than the shiny plate when exposed to sunlight.
parts.markscheme:
- Black surfaces are better absorbers of thermal radiation <marks>1 mark</marks>

- When exposed to sunlight, black plate absorbs more radiation than shiny plate <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Shiny surface reflects most of incoming solar radiation, leading to less absorption and slower heating <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify thermal transfer mechanism
steps.content: To answer this, we need to look at **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. The heat from the sun reaches the plates via thermal radiation. How a material responds to this radiation depends entirely on its surface properties.

steps.title: Define absorption properties
steps.content: In physics, we classify surfaces based on their ability to absorb or reflect energy. A key principle is that dark, matt surfaces—like the black plate—are excellent absorbers of thermal radiation. This means they are very efficient at taking in the energy carried by sunlight.

steps.title: Contrast with shiny surfaces
steps.content: While the black plate absorbs radiation, the shiny plate behaves differently. Shiny or light-colored surfaces are poor absorbers because they are excellent reflectors. When sunlight hits the shiny plate, most of the incoming solar radiation is reflected away rather than being absorbed into the metal.

steps.title: Relate absorption to temperature
steps.content:
Since the black plate absorbs a much higher rate of energy, its internal energy increases faster than that of the shiny plate. We can see the relationship between energy added ($Q$) and temperature change ($\Delta T$) in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Because the black plate receives a larger amount of thermal energy ($Q$) in the same amount of time, it results in a higher temperature increase ($\Delta T$), making it feel noticeably warmer.

steps.title: Identify surface properties
steps.content: To understand why different surfaces heat up at different rates, we look at their **albedo** and **reflectivity**. When sunlight hits an object, the energy is either reflected away or absorbed. This interaction is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers** and **Topic B.2: Greenhouse effect**.

steps.title: Analyze the shiny surface
steps.content: A **shiny metal plate** has a very high reflectivity (high albedo). This means that when it is exposed to direct sunlight, it reflects most of the incoming solar radiation back into the surroundings rather than allowing it to be absorbed into the material.

highlights.text: Shiny surface reflects most of incoming solar radiation, leading to less absorption and slower heating

steps.title: Analyze the black surface
steps.content: In contrast, a **black metal plate** has a very low reflectivity and a low albedo. Black surfaces are naturally excellent absorbers of thermal radiation. Because it reflects very little, the majority of the incident solar energy is absorbed, increasing the internal energy of the plate.

highlights.text: Black surfaces are better absorbers of thermal radiation

steps.title: Relate absorption to heating
steps.content: Because the black plate absorbs a significantly higher fraction of the solar radiation compared to the shiny plate, it gains thermal energy much faster. This leads to a more rapid increase in temperature, explaining why the black plate becomes noticeably warmer over the same period of time.

highlights.text: black plate absorbs more radiation than shiny plate

parts.content: If both plates are then moved to a shaded area, explain how the color and surface properties of each plate affect how quickly they lose heat.
parts.markscheme:
- Both plates lose heat by emitting thermal radiation to surroundings <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Black plate is a good emitter and radiates heat more efficiently than shiny plate (poor emitter due to reflective surface) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Black plate cools down more quickly in shade due to being a good emitter <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Shiny plate retains heat longer and cools more slowly due to poor emission of thermal radiation <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award marks for clear comparison between plates and explicit linking of surface properties to cooling rates</note>

steps.title: Identify heat loss mechanism
steps.content:
When the plates are moved to the shaded area, they are warmer than their surroundings. The primary mechanism by which they transfer this thermal energy to the environment is through the emission of **thermal radiation** (infrared radiation). 

This concept is a key part of **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

steps.title: Compare emission properties
steps.content:
The rate at which an object radiates heat depends heavily on its surface properties. 

*   **Black surfaces** are excellent **emitters** of thermal radiation.
*   **Shiny or silvery surfaces** are **poor emitters** (they are good reflectors but do not radiate energy away effectively).

steps.title: Determine the cooling rates
steps.content:
Because the black plate is a more efficient emitter, it loses its internal thermal energy to the surroundings at a much higher rate. 

Consequently, the **black plate will cool down more quickly** than the shiny plate.

steps.title: Conclusion for shiny plate
steps.content: In contrast, the shiny plate's low emissivity means it radiates heat very slowly. Therefore, the **shiny plate will retain its heat for a longer period** and its temperature will decrease at a much slower rate compared to the black plate.

steps.title: Identify the cooling mechanism
steps.content:
When the plates are moved to a shaded area, they are no longer receiving direct energy from the sun. Instead, they begin to lose their internal energy to the cooler surroundings. The primary mechanism for this heat loss is the emission of **thermal radiation** (infrared radiation).

According to the markscheme, identifying this mechanism is essential for the first mark.

steps.title: Apply Absorption-Emission Reciprocity
steps.content:
In **Topic B.1 (Thermal energy transfers)**, we learn about the properties of surfaces. A fundamental principle of physics is that **good absorbers of radiation are also good emitters of radiation**.

From the first part of the experiment, we saw that the black plate was noticeably warmer because it was a better absorber of sunlight. Because of this reciprocity, we can conclude that the black plate will also be a much more efficient **emitter** of thermal radiation compared to the shiny plate.

highlights.text: black plate is noticeably warmer than the shiny plate

steps.title: Compare the surface properties
steps.content:
We can now distinguish between the two surfaces:

- **Black surface:** Its dark, matte texture allows it to radiate heat energy away very efficiently. It is a **good emitter**.
- **Shiny surface:** Its reflective properties make it a **poor emitter**. Just as it reflects incoming light, it is "bad" at letting internal thermal energy escape via radiation.

This comparison earns the second mark.

steps.title: Determine cooling rates
steps.content:
Finally, we link the emission efficiency to the rate of cooling:

- Because the **black plate** is a good emitter, it loses energy to the surroundings at a high rate, meaning it **cools down quickly**.
- Because the **shiny plate** is a poor emitter, it retains its heat for a longer period and **cools down more slowly**.

Explaining these specific cooling rates for both plates completes the final two marks.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.1 Thermal energy transfers > B.1.3 Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.3 Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses Stefan–Boltzmann law for thermal radiation power, depending on surface area (∝r^2) and temperature (∝T^4), which falls under mechanisms of thermal energy transfer (radiation).

options.markscheme: The student may have neglected to square the radius ($3 \times \frac{1}{3^2} = \frac{1}{3}$) or mistakenly used a lower power for the temperature.

The total power radiated $P$ is given by the Stefan-Boltzmann law $P = e \sigma A T^4$. Since the bodies are spherical, $A = 4\pi R^2$, so $P \propto R^2 T^4$. For body X, $P_X \propto (3R_Y)^2 \times (\frac{1}{3}T_Y)^4 = 9R_Y^2 \times \frac{1}{81}T_Y^4 = \frac{9}{81} P_Y = \frac{1}{9}P$.
The student may have used an incorrect power for the temperature or radius relationship.
The student correctly accounted for the temperature change ($(\frac{1}{3})^4 = \frac{1}{81}$) but neglected the change in surface area due to the different radii.

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content: To calculate the thermal energy ($Q$) absorbed by the block, we use the specific heat capacity equation from the **Thermal Physics** section of the data booklet. This formula relates heat energy, mass, specific heat capacity, and temperature change.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
Extract the known values from the question text. Note that a temperature change in degrees Celsius ($^\circ\text{C}$) is numerically equal to the change in Kelvin (K).

*   Mass: $$m = 2.00\ \text{kg}$$
*   Specific heat capacity: $$c = 900\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$$
*   Temperature change: $$\Delta T = 10.0^\circ\text{C} = 10.0\ \text{K}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into a new line:

calculator.description: Calculate the product of mass, specific heat, and temperature change.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The total thermal energy absorbed is:

$$Q = 18000\ \text{J}$$

parts.content: State one assumption made in this calculation.
parts.markscheme: No heat is lost to the surroundings / all energy goes into heating the block <marks>B1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
The calculation relies on the specific heat capacity equation from **Topic B.1 Thermal energy transfers**. This formula relates the energy transferred to the mass and temperature change:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

To use this formula effectively in a calculation, specific conditions must be met regarding how energy flows.

steps.title: Apply Isolated System Assumption
steps.content:
In a real experiment, a heated object naturally transfers thermal energy to cooler objects nearby, such as the air or the surface it rests on. However, this calculation uses the **Isolated System Assumption**.

This approach assumes the system is ideal and perfectly insulated, meaning the energy transfer is 100% efficient.

steps.title: State the assumption
steps.content:
Therefore, the assumption made is that **no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings**.

This means we assume all the energy supplied goes exclusively into increasing the internal energy (temperature) of the metal block.

parts.content: Identify whether this energy change is associated with kinetic or potential energy of the particles.
parts.markscheme: The energy change is associated with the **kinetic energy** of the particles <marks>B1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the definition of temperature
steps.content:
In IB Physics, it is fundamental to understand what temperature represents on a microscopic level. Temperature is defined as a measure of the **average random kinetic energy** of the particles in a substance.

This definition applies to the particles in solids, liquids, and gases.

steps.title: Analyze the change in the block
steps.content:
The question states that the block is heated and the **temperature increases**. 

Because temperature is a direct measure of kinetic energy, an increase in temperature means the particles (atoms in the metal lattice) are vibrating faster. Therefore, the average kinetic energy of these particles is increasing.

steps.title: Identify the energy type
steps.content:
Since the temperature rise is directly linked to the speed of particle vibrations, the energy change described is associated with the **kinetic energy** of the particles.

(Note: While the potential energy of the bonds also changes as the solid expands, the specific link to *temperature* is through kinetic energy).

steps.title: Analyze the given information
steps.content: The question states that the metal block is heated and undergoes a temperature rise.

steps.title: Distinguish between energy types
steps.content:
Using the **Distinction from Phase Change** approach, we recall how internal energy components behave during heating:

*   **Potential Energy ($E_p$):** Changes during a **phase change** (like melting or boiling) where bonds break or form at a constant temperature. This corresponds to the Latent Heat formula in the data booklet: $$Q=mL$$
*   **Kinetic Energy ($E_k$):** Changes during a **temperature change**, as temperature is a measure of the average random kinetic energy of the particles. This corresponds to the Specific Heat formula: $$Q=mc\Delta T$$

steps.title: Identify the energy change
steps.content:
Since the problem specifies that the temperature increases and there is no mention of a phase change (the block remains a solid metal), the energy supplied results in the particles vibrating faster.

Therefore, the energy change is associated with the **kinetic energy** of the particles.

steps.title: Link temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the kinetic theory of gases, the absolute temperature of a gas is directly proportional to the average random kinetic energy of its particles. This relationship is shown in the data booklet as:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

This means that as the temperature $T$ increases, the average kinetic energy $\overline{E_{k}}$ of the gas particles must also increase.

steps.title: Relate to internal energy
steps.content:
In IB Physics, the **internal energy** of a substance is defined as the sum of the random kinetic energy and the intermolecular potential energy of its particles. 

For an ideal gas, we assume there are no intermolecular forces, so the internal energy consists entirely of the total random kinetic energy of the particles.

steps.title: Conclude the effect
steps.content:
Because an increase in temperature leads to an increase in the average kinetic energy of the particles, the total internal energy of the gas will also increase.

To secure both marks, ensure you:
1. State that the **average kinetic energy** of the particles increases.
2. Conclude that the **internal energy** of the gas increases.

highlights.text: As temperature increases, the average kinetic energy of the particles increases
This leads to an increase in the internal energy of the gas

steps.title: Define internal energy
steps.content:
To answer this, we first need to recall the definition of internal energy from **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. 

The internal energy of a substance is the sum of the total random kinetic energy of the particles and the intermolecular potential energy between them.

steps.title: Relate temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the kinetic theory of gases, the average random kinetic energy of the particles is directly proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas. This is shown by the formula in the data booklet:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Therefore, when the **temperature increases**, the average kinetic energy of the gas particles also increases. This accounts for the first mark.

steps.title: Conclude internal energy change
steps.content:
Since internal energy is the sum of the kinetic and potential components, an increase in the total random kinetic energy (with the potential energy remaining constant or negligible for an ideal gas) results in an overall increase in internal energy.

Final Answer: The **internal energy increases** because the average kinetic energy of the particles increases with temperature.

parts.content: State what is meant by thermal conductivity.
parts.markscheme:
Thermal conductivity is a material property that measures how well it conducts heat <marks>A1</marks>

Defined as the rate of thermal energy transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define as a property
steps.content:
In physics, we treat thermal conductivity as an intrinsic **material property**. This means it doesn't depend on the size or shape of the object, but rather on the substance itself. 

It characterizes how effectively or "easily" a substance can conduct thermal energy from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature. For example, metals generally have high thermal conductivity, while insulators like wood or foam have low thermal conductivity.

highlights.text: Thermal conductivity is a material property that measures how well it conducts heat

steps.title: Explain the formal definition
steps.content:
To be more precise, thermal conductivity is defined by how much energy moves through the material under specific conditions. It is the **rate of thermal energy transfer** (the amount of heat per second) that passes through a **unit cross-sectional area** when there is a **unit temperature gradient** (change in temperature per unit length).

This tells us that for a given temperature difference across a material, a higher thermal conductivity results in a faster flow of heat.

highlights.text: Defined as the rate of thermal energy transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient

steps.title: General property
steps.content: Thermal conductivity is a fundamental material property that indicates how well a substance conducts heat. In the context of your study of **Topic B.1 (Thermal energy transfers)**, it describes the efficiency with which thermal energy moves through a solid by the vibration of atoms and the movement of free electrons.

highlights.text: Thermal conductivity is a material property that measures how well it conducts heat

steps.title: Quantitative physical definition
steps.content:
To define it quantitatively, we look at the relationship between the rate of heat flow and the physical dimensions of the material. Thermal conductivity is defined as the **rate of thermal energy transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient**.

We can see this relationship in the conduction formula:

$$\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = k A \frac{\Delta T}{L}$$

By rearranging this to solve for thermal conductivity ($k$), we get:

$$k = \frac{\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}}{A \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{L}}$$

This shows that $k$ is the heat flow rate ($W$) per unit area ($m^2$) for every $1 \text{ K/m}$ of temperature gradient.

highlights.text: Defined as the rate of thermal energy transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the IB markscheme, your definition should include both the general property and the specific quantitative relationship:

1. It is a material property measuring how well a substance conducts heat.
2. It is the rate of thermal energy transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient.

parts.content: Outline why, at steady state, the rate of thermal energy flow through each block must be equal.
parts.markscheme:
In steady state, there is no accumulation of thermal energy at the junction <marks>R1</marks>

Therefore, the rate of energy entering the junction from the hot side equals the rate leaving it to the cold side <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Define steady state
steps.content: To understand this, we first need to define what **steady state** means. In thermal physics, a system reaches a steady state when its properties, such as the temperature at any specific point, no longer change with time. This concept is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

highlights.text: The system reaches a steady state

steps.title: Analyze the junction
steps.content: Let's focus on the interface where the two blocks meet, often called the **junction**. Because the system is in a steady state, the temperature at this junction is constant. For the temperature to remain constant, there can be **no accumulation of thermal energy** at that point.

steps.title: Apply energy balance
steps.content:
Using the principle of conservation of energy, if the energy isn't accumulating, the rate at which energy arrives at the junction from the hot block must exactly equal the rate at which it leaves through the cold block.

$$\text{Rate of energy in} = \text{Rate of energy out}$$

If these rates were different, the junction would either heat up or cool down, which would contradict the definition of a steady state.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: Therefore, to maintain a constant temperature profile across the composite system, the rate of thermal energy flow (the power) must be uniform through both blocks.

steps.title: Define steady state
steps.content: To understand why the flow rates are equal, we first need to recall what "steady state" means in physics. In a **steady state**, the temperature at any specific point in the system remains constant over time. This concept is fundamental to **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

steps.title: Analyze the junction
steps.content:
Consider the junction where the two blocks meet. Thermal energy flows from the hot end ($125^\circ\mathrm{C}$) through the first block, enters the junction, and then leaves through the second block toward the cold end ($15^\circ\mathrm{C}$). 

Let $P_{in}$ be the rate of energy entering the junction and $P_{out}$ be the rate of energy leaving it.

steps.title: Use the stability argument
steps.content:
Imagine if these rates were *not* equal. If $P_{in}$ were greater than $P_{out}$, energy would accumulate at the junction. 

According to the formula for specific heat capacity:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

An accumulation of energy ($Q$) would result in a temperature increase ($\Delta T$) at that point. Conversely, if $P_{out}$ were greater than $P_{in}$, the junction would lose energy and its temperature would drop.

steps.title: Conclude from contradictions
steps.content:
However, we are told the system is in a **steady state**, which means the temperature at the junction cannot change. 

For the temperature to remain stable, there must be **no net accumulation of energy**. Therefore, the rate of thermal energy entering the junction must exactly equal the rate of energy leaving it, meaning the flow rate through both blocks is identical.

parts.content:
Show that the temperature $T$ at the junction satisfies the equation:
$$2(125 - T) = 3(T - 15)$$
parts.markscheme:
State or use rate of thermal energy transfer formula: $\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute values for both blocks and equate: $\frac{2kA(125-T)}{L} = \frac{3kA(T-15)}{L}$ <marks>A1</marks>

Cancel common terms $k, A, L$ to obtain the equation <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: State conduction formula
steps.content:
To solve this, we use the rate of thermal energy transfer (power) by conduction. This is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. The formula for the rate of heat flow, $P$, through a material is:

$$P = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$$

Where:
- $k$ is the thermal conductivity
- $A$ is the cross-sectional area
- $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference across the block
- $L$ is the length of the block

steps.title: Express rate for Block 1
steps.content:
Let's look at the first block on the left. We are given its thermal conductivity is $2k$. The temperature drops from the hot end ($125^\circ\mathrm{C}$) to the junction ($T$). 

The rate of energy transfer for this block, $P_1$, is:

$$P_1 = \frac{(2k)A(125 - T)}{L}$$

highlights.text: thermal conductivities of the blocks are 2k and 3k

steps.title: Express rate for Block 2
steps.content:
Now for the second block on the right. Its thermal conductivity is $3k$. The temperature drops from the junction ($T$) to the cold end ($15^\circ\mathrm{C}$).

The rate of energy transfer for this block, $P_2$, is:

$$P_2 = \frac{(3k)A(T - 15)}{L}$$

steps.title: Apply steady-state condition
steps.content:
When the system reaches a **steady state**, the rate of thermal energy entering the junction from the first block must equal the rate leaving through the second block. This means $P_1 = P_2$.

$$\frac{2kA(125 - T)}{L} = \frac{3kA(T - 15)}{L}$$

steps.title: Simplify the equation
steps.content:
To reach the final expression, we cancel the common terms on both sides. Since both blocks have the same area $A$ and length $L$, and $k$ is a common factor:

1. Divide both sides by $k$
2. Divide both sides by $A$
3. Multiply both sides by $L$

This leaves us with:

$$2(125 - T) = 3(T - 15)$$ 

This matches the equation we were asked to show!

steps.title: Calculate junction temperature
steps.content:
Although not required by the "show that" question, you might need to find the actual temperature $T$. We can expand the brackets and solve for $T$:

$$\begin{aligned} 250 - 2T &= 3T - 45 \\ 295 &= 5T \\ T &= 59^\circ\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify steady state condition
steps.content:
In a series system at **steady state**, the rate of thermal energy flow (power) through each component must be identical. If it weren't, energy would build up at the junction, and the temperature would change.

This concept is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. We use the formula for the rate of heat transfer:

$$\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$$

highlights.text: The system reaches a steady state
rate of thermal energy transfer formula: $\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$

steps.title: Establish the conductivity ratio
steps.content:
Since the rate of energy transfer is the same for both blocks, and we are told they have the same cross-sectional area ($A$) and the same length ($L$), we can simplify our comparison. 

For two blocks in series with identical dimensions:
$$k_1 \Delta T_1 = k_2 \Delta T_2$$

This shows an inverse relationship: $\Delta T \propto \frac{1}{k}$. The block with lower conductivity will require a larger temperature difference to pass the same amount of heat.

highlights.text: equal cross-sectional area and equal length $L$

steps.title: Identify variables for each block
steps.content:
Let's define the variables for each block based on the diagram and the question text:

**Block 1 (Hot side):**
- Thermal conductivity: $k_1 = 2k$
- Temperature difference: $\Delta T_1 = (125 - T)$

**Block 2 (Cold side):**
- Thermal conductivity: $k_2 = 3k$
- Temperature difference: $\Delta T_2 = (T - 15)$

highlights.text: thermal conductivities of the blocks are $2k$ and $3k$

steps.title: Derive the final equation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute these values into our ratio relationship:

$$\begin{aligned} k_1 \Delta T_1 &= k_2 \Delta T_2 \\ 2k(125 - T) &= 3k(T - 15) \end{aligned}$$

Since the constant $k$ appears on both sides, we can divide the entire equation by $k$ to cancel it out:

$$2(125 - T) = 3(T - 15)$$

This matches the equation required by the question.

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: State the SI unit for the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, $\sigma$.
parts.markscheme: - $\mathrm{W} \, \mathrm{m}^{-2} \, \mathrm{K}^{-4}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The following information is available for an asteroid in deep space.

Incident intensity of solar radiation = $1500 \mathrm{~W} \, \mathrm{m}^{-2}$
Albedo of the asteroid = $0.15$
Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} \, \mathrm{m}^{-2} \, \mathrm{K}^{-4}$

Calculate the average equilibrium temperature of the asteroid's surface.

Assume the asteroid acts as a black body for emission and that thermal energy is distributed uniformly over its spherical surface.
parts.markscheme:
- State the condition for equilibrium: Power absorbed = Power radiated  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Determine the average absorbed intensity over the total surface area:
$I_{\text{absorbed}} = \frac{1500 \times (1 - 0.15)}{4} = 318.75 \mathrm{~W} \, \mathrm{m}^{-2}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Set up the equation using the Stefan-Boltzmann law:
$318.75 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times T^4$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Solve for $T$:
$T = \sqrt[4]{\frac{318.75}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}}$
$T \approx 274 \mathrm{~K}$ (or $1 ^{\circ}\text{C}$)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the number of moles of gas in the container.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitution into mole formula: $n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1.20}{0.040}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct final answer with units: $30.0\ \text{mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To find the number of moles ($n$), we first need to identify the total mass of the gas and its molar mass from the question. 

We are given:
- Total mass ($m$) = $1.20\ \text{kg}$
- Molar mass ($M$) = $0.040\ \text{kg mol}^{-1}$

Note that both values are already in SI units (kilograms), so no unit conversion is necessary!

steps.title: Apply the mole formula
steps.content:
In **Topic B.3 (Gas laws)**, we learn that the number of moles is the ratio of the total mass of a substance to its molar mass. We use the following relationship:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Where:
- $n$ is the number of moles
- $m$ is the total mass
- $M$ is the molar mass

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{1.20\ \text{kg}}{0.040\ \text{kg mol}^{-1}} \\ n &= 30.0\ \text{mol} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, showing this substitution earns the first mark (**M1**), and providing the correct final answer with units earns the second mark (**A1**).

First, we need to find the amount of gas in moles ($n$). We can calculate this using the given mass ($m$) and the molar mass ($M$) of the gas.

steps.title: Identify the internal energy formula
steps.content:
For an **ideal monatomic gas**, the total internal energy ($U$) is simply the total kinetic energy of all the particles. Using the kinetic theory equation $\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$ combined with the ideal gas law relation, we use the molar version of the internal energy formula:

$$U = \frac{3}{2}nRT$$

This formula relates internal energy directly to the number of moles ($n$), the gas constant ($R$), and the absolute temperature ($T$).

steps.title: Substitute values into the formula
steps.content:
Now we substitute our calculated moles ($n = 30.0$), the universal gas constant ($R$), and the temperature ($T$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{3}{2} (30.0)(8.31)(300.0) \\ U &= 1.5 \times 30.0 \times 8.31 \times 300.0 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total internal energy

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the multiplication gives the total internal energy in Joules. The result should be rounded to 3 significant figures to match the precision of the given data.

$$U = 112185\ \text{J} \approx 1.12 \times 10^5\ \text{J}$$

- Correct final change in internal energy: $\Delta U = \frac{3}{2}nR\Delta T = \frac{3}{2} \times 30.0 \times 8.31 \times 300.0 = 1.12 \times 10^5\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate the number of moles
steps.content:
First, we need to find the amount of gas in moles ($n$). We do this by dividing the total mass ($m$) by the molar mass ($M$):

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$n = \frac{1.20\text{ kg}}{0.040\text{ kg mol}^{-1}} = 30.0\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Find the temperature change
steps.content:
The question states the gas is heated until its temperature doubles. We can find the final temperature ($T_{final}$) and then calculate the change in temperature ($\Delta T$):

$$\begin{aligned} T_{initial} &= 300.0\text{ K} \\ T_{final} &= 2 \times 300.0\text{ K} = 600.0\text{ K} \\ \Delta T &= T_{final} - T_{initial} \\ \Delta T &= 600.0\text{ K} - 300.0\text{ K} = 300.0\text{ K} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate internal energy change
steps.content:
For a monatomic ideal gas, the change in internal energy ($\Delta U$) is proportional to the change in temperature. We use the formula:

$$\Delta U = \frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T$$

Substituting our values ($n = 30.0$, $R = 8.31$, and $\Delta T = 300.0$):

$$\Delta U = 1.5 \times 30.0 \times 8.31 \times 300.0$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final change in internal energy.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculated change in internal energy is:

$$\Delta U = 112185\text{ J}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$\Delta U = 1.12 \times 10^5\text{ J}$$

steps.title: Calculate the number of moles
steps.content:
To find the internal energy, we first need to know the amount of gas in moles ($n$). We use the relationship between mass ($m$) and molar mass ($M$):

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Given the mass is $1.20\ \text{kg}$ and the molar mass is $0.040\ \text{kg mol}^{-1}$:

$$n = \frac{1.20}{0.040} = 30.0\ \text{mol}$$

steps.title: Determine the final temperature
steps.content:
The question states the gas is heated until its temperature doubles. We identify the initial temperature ($T_1$) and calculate the final temperature ($T_2$):

$$T_1 = 300.0\ \text{K}$$
$$T_2 = 2 \times 300.0 = 600.0\ \text{K}$$

steps.title: Calculate initial internal energy
steps.content:
The internal energy ($U$) of an ideal monatomic gas is given by the formula:

$$U = \frac{3}{2}nRT$$

Substituting the initial values ($n = 30.0$, $R = 8.31$, $T_1 = 300.0$):

$$U_{\text{initial}} = \frac{3}{2} \times 30.0 \times 8.31 \times 300.0 = 112,185\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 1.5 by 30, then by 8.31, then by 300.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial internal energy state.

steps.title: Calculate final internal energy
steps.content:
Now we calculate the internal energy at the final temperature ($T_2 = 600.0\ \text{K}$):

$$U_{\text{final}} = \frac{3}{2} \times 30.0 \times 8.31 \times 600.0 = 224,370\ \text{J}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 1.5 by 30, then by 8.31, then by 600.

calculator.description: Calculate the final internal energy state.

steps.title: Find the energy change
steps.content:
The change in internal energy ($\Delta U$) is the difference between the final and initial states:

$$\Delta U = U_{\text{final}} - U_{\text{initial}}$$
$$\Delta U = 224,370 - 112,185 = 112,185\ \text{J}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$\Delta U = 1.12 \times 10^5\ \text{J}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the initial energy from the final energy.

steps.title: Determine the temperature change
steps.content:
The question states that the gas is heated until its temperature doubles. Since the initial temperature is $300.0\ \text{K}$, the final temperature and the change in temperature ($\Delta T$) are:

$$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{final}} &= 2 \times 300.0 = 600.0\ \text{K} \\ \Delta T &= 600.0 - 300.0 = 300.0\ \text{K} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate total gas particles
steps.content:
To use the microscopic approach, we first find the total number of particles ($N$). We start by calculating the number of moles ($n$) using the given mass and molar mass:

$$n = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{1.20\ \text{kg}}{0.040\ \text{kg mol}^{-1}} = 30.0\ \text{mol}$$

Then, we multiply by Avogadro's constant ($N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$):

$$N = n \times N_A = 30.0 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} = 1.806 \times 10^{25}\ \text{particles}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles.

steps.title: Find energy change per particle
steps.content:
In an ideal monatomic gas, the internal energy is entirely kinetic. The average kinetic energy per particle is given by the formula in the data booklet. The change in average kinetic energy ($\Delta \overline{E_k}$) for a temperature change $\Delta T$ is:

$$\Delta \overline{E_k} = \frac{3}{2} k_B \Delta T$$

Using the Boltzmann constant ($k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J K}^{-1}$):

$$\Delta \overline{E_k} = 1.5 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300.0 = 6.21 \times 10^{-21}\ \text{J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the change in average kinetic energy per particle.

steps.title: Calculate total internal energy change
steps.content:
Finally, the total change in internal energy ($\Delta U$) is the number of particles multiplied by the energy change per particle:

$$\Delta U = N \times \Delta \overline{E_k}$$
$$\Delta U = (1.806 \times 10^{25}) \times (6.21 \times 10^{-21})$$
$$\Delta U \approx 1.12 \times 10^5\ \text{J}$$

calculator.description: Multiply the number of particles by the energy change per particle.

parts.content: Explain in terms of particle motion what happens to the internal energy when the temperature of an ideal gas increases.
parts.markscheme:
- State that an increase in temperature increases the average kinetic energy of gas particles <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that for an ideal gas, internal energy is entirely kinetic, so it increases proportionally with temperature <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Relate temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
First, we look at the relationship between temperature and the motion of the particles. According to the kinetic theory, the absolute temperature is directly proportional to the average random kinetic energy of the particles. We can see this in the data booklet equation:

$$\overline{E_{k}} = \frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

This means that as the temperature ($T$) increases, the average kinetic energy ($\overline{E_{k}}$) of the particles increases, implying the particles move with greater average speed.

steps.title: Define internal energy for an ideal gas
steps.content:
Next, we consider the definition of internal energy. Generally, internal energy is the sum of the total random kinetic energy and total potential energy of the particles. However, the question specifies an **ideal gas**.

For an ideal gas, there are no intermolecular forces, which means the potential energy is zero. Therefore, the internal energy consists **entirely** of the total kinetic energy of the particles.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Since an increase in temperature causes the average kinetic energy of the particles to increase, the **total** kinetic energy of the gas also increases. Because the internal energy of an ideal gas is equal to this total kinetic energy, the internal energy of the system increases.

steps.title: Identify the required formula
steps.content:
To calculate the energy required to change the temperature of a substance without a phase change, we use the specific heat capacity formula found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc	riangle T$$

Where:
- $Q$ is the energy transferred
- $m$ is the mass
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity
- $\triangle T$ is the change in temperature

steps.title: Calculate the temperature change
steps.content:
Next, we determine the temperature change $\triangle T$. The question asks for the energy needed to heat the ice from $-10.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $0^{\circ}\text{C}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \triangle T &= T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}} \\ \triangle T &= 0.0^{\circ}\text{C} - (-10.0^{\circ}\text{C}) \\ \triangle T &= 10.0^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned}$$

In IB Physics, remember that a temperature *change* of $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ is exactly equal to a change of $1\text{ K}$, so we can use $\triangle T = 10.0\text{ K}$ in our calculation.

steps.title: Calculate the energy required
steps.content:
We can now substitute the mass of the ice, the specific heat capacity of ice, and our calculated temperature change into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= 0.150\text{ kg} \times 2100\text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1} \times 10.0\text{ K} \\ Q &= 3150\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This direct calculation gives us the total energy required for this specific heating phase. According to the markscheme, showing the substitution earns the first mark (M1), and the final value with units earns the second (A1).

methods.label: Direct Calculation using Specific Heat Formula

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To calculate the energy required to change the temperature of a substance without changing its state, we use the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q$ is the thermal energy (J)
- $m$ is the mass (kg)
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity ($\text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$)
- $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature (K or $^{\circ}\text{C}$)

steps.title: List the given values
steps.content:
From the question, we can identify the specific values needed for the calculation. Since we are heating liquid water, we must use the specific heat capacity for water ($c_{\text{water}}$).

- Mass ($m$) = $0.150 \text{ kg}$
- Specific heat capacity ($c_{\text{water}}$) = $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$
- Initial temperature ($T_i$) = $0^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Final temperature ($T_f$) = $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$

steps.title: Calculate temperature change
steps.content:
First, find the change in temperature ($\Delta T$):

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta T &= T_f - T_i \\ \Delta T &= 100.0^{\circ}\text{C} - 0^{\circ}\text{C} \\ \Delta T &= 100.0 \text{ K} \end{aligned}$$

Note: A change of $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1 \text{ K}$.

steps.title: Calculate energy required
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the formula to find the energy $Q$:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= mc\Delta T \\ Q &= 0.150 \times 4200 \times 100.0 \\ Q &= 63000 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

You would earn one mark for the correct substitution (M1) and one mark for the correct final answer with units (A1).

steps.title: Identify the required values
steps.content:
To calculate the energy required to heat the steam, we first need to identify the mass of the sample, the specific heat capacity of steam, and the temperature range.

From the question, we have:
- Mass ($m$) = $0.150 \text{ kg}$
- Specific heat capacity of steam ($c_{\text{steam}}$) = $2000 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$
- Initial temperature = $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Final temperature = $120.0^{\circ}\text{C}$

steps.title: Determine the temperature change
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the change in temperature ($\Delta T$). In IB Physics, remember that a change of $1^{\circ}\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\text{ K}$, so we can use the values in Celsius directly:

$$\Delta T = 120.0^{\circ}\text{C} - 100.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 20.0\text{ K}$$

steps.title: Apply specific heat formula
steps.content:
Since we are changing the temperature of a single phase (steam) without a phase change, we use the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Substituting our known values:

$$Q = 0.150 \times 2000 \times 20.0$$

steps.title: Calculate the final energy
steps.content:
Now, we perform the multiplication to find the total energy required. 

$$Q = 6000 \text{ J}$$

This calculation earns two marks: one for the correct substitution of values ($0.150 \times 2000 \times 20.0$) and one for the final answer with the correct unit (Joules).

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the physical stages
steps.content:
To find the total energy required to turn ice at $-10.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ into steam at $120.0^{\circ}\text{C}$, we must break the process into five distinct physical stages. This aligns with **Topic B.1.2: Phase changes and energy transfer**. 

1.  **Heating Ice**: $-10.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $0.0^{\circ}\text{C}$
2.  **Melting Ice**: At $0.0^{\circ}\text{C}$
3.  **Heating Water**: $0.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$
4.  **Boiling Water**: At $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$
5.  **Heating Steam**: $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $120.0^{\circ}\text{C}$

steps.title: Stage 1: Heating ice
steps.content:
First, we calculate the energy needed to heat $0.150 \text{ kg}$ of ice from $-10.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to its melting point at $0.0^{\circ}\text{C}$. We use the specific heat capacity formula from the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Substituting the values:
$$Q_1 = 0.150 \times 2100 \times (0 - (-10))$$
$$Q_1 = 3150 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Stage 2: Melting ice
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the energy required to melt the ice completely at $0.0^{\circ}\text{C}$. Since the temperature doesn't change during a phase change, we use the latent heat formula:

$$Q = mL$$

Substituting the values for fusion:
$$Q_2 = 0.150 \times 3.34 \times 10^5$$
$$Q_2 = 50100 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Stage 3: Heating water
steps.content:
Now we heat the liquid water from $0.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to its boiling point at $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$Q_3 = mc_{\text{water}}\Delta T$$
$$Q_3 = 0.150 \times 4200 \times (100 - 0)$$
$$Q_3 = 63000 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Stage 4: Boiling water
steps.content:
We calculate the energy required to vaporize the water into steam at $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$Q_4 = mL_{\text{v}}$$
$$Q_4 = 0.150 \times 2.26 \times 10^6$$
$$Q_4 = 339000 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Stage 5: Heating steam
steps.content:
Finally, we heat the steam from $100.0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $120.0^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$Q_5 = mc_{\text{steam}}\Delta T$$
$$Q_5 = 0.150 \times 2000 \times (120 - 100)$$
$$Q_5 = 6000 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Calculate the total energy
steps.content:
To find the final answer, we sum the energy values calculated for all five stages:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5$$
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 3150 + 50100 + 63000 + 339000 + 6000$$
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 461250 \text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the sum of the five values into the main screen.

ti84.instructions: Type the sum of the calculated values.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of energy for all stages.

parts.content: A student uses the average rate of cooling over the 10-minute interval to estimate the power loss for Beaker B at $t = 10$ minutes. State and explain how the changing rate of cooling affects the accuracy of this estimate.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the rate of cooling decreases as the temperature difference with the surroundings decreases, meaning the average rate will be an overestimate of the instantaneous rate at the end of the interval. <marks>R1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Link rate to temperature difference
steps.content: The rate at which thermal energy is transferred from an object to its surroundings depends on the temperature difference between them. In this case, the water starts at $80\,^\circ\text{C}$ in a room that is $20\,^\circ\text{C}$. This concept is part of **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

steps.title: Identify the cooling trend
steps.content: As the water cools, the temperature difference between the water and the room decreases. Because the rate of cooling is proportional to this temperature difference, the rate is not constant; it actually slows down over time. We can see this on the graph as the gradient (slope) of the curve for Beaker B becomes less steep as time passes.

steps.title: Compare average and instantaneous rates
steps.content:
The student uses the **average rate of cooling** over the first 10 minutes. This average includes the very high cooling rates from the start of the experiment. 

At $t = 10$ minutes, the water has reached its lowest temperature in the interval, meaning the temperature difference is at its minimum. Therefore, the **instantaneous rate** of cooling at $10$ minutes is much lower than the average rate calculated over the whole period.

steps.title: Determine the estimate accuracy
steps.content: Since the average rate is higher than the actual rate at the 10-minute mark, using the average to calculate power loss ($P = \frac{Q}{t} = mc\frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$) will result in an **overestimate** of the power loss at that specific time.

steps.title: Analyze the cooling curve
steps.content: Looking at the graph for **Beaker B**, we can see that it is not a straight line. Instead, it is a curve that becomes less steep as time progresses. This happens because the rate of cooling depends on the temperature difference between the water and the room ($20\,^\circ\text{C}$). As the water cools, this difference decreases, and so does the rate of thermal energy transfer.

steps.title: Compare secant and tangent
steps.content:
In graphical terms:
- The **average rate** over the 10-minute interval is represented by the slope of the **secant line** (a straight line connecting the start point at $t=0$ and the end point at $t=10$).
- The **instantaneous rate** at $t=10$ is represented by the slope of the **tangent line** at exactly the 10-minute mark.

Because the curve is "concave up" (it curves away from the x-axis), the slope of the secant line is steeper than the slope of the tangent line at the end of the interval.

highlights.text: average rate of cooling over the 10-minute interval

steps.title: Evaluate the estimate
steps.content:
Since the slope of the secant line (average rate) is steeper than the slope of the tangent line (instantaneous rate), the average rate is a higher value than the actual rate at $t=10$.

Therefore, using the average rate results in an **overestimate** of the power loss at the 10-minute mark.

parts.content: State how the temperature of the block changes over time.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the temperature increases steadily over time with a slight decrease in rate <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the general direction
steps.content:
Using **Visual Trend Analysis**, first look at the overall direction of the line of best fit.

As you move from left to right along the horizontal axis (time), the line goes **upwards**. This indicates that the **temperature increases** over time.

steps.title: Inspect the curve shape
steps.content:
Next, look at the **steepness** (gradient) of the line.

- At the beginning, the line is steeper.
- Towards the end (around 4-5 minutes), the line becomes slightly flatter (less steep).

This "concave down" shape means that the **rate of temperature increase is decreasing** over time.

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
Combine these observations to state the final answer as required by the markscheme:

The temperature **increases** over time, but the **rate of increase decreases** (the temperature rises at a slower rate as time goes on).

steps.title: Method: Calculating Rate Changes
steps.content: To accurately describe how the temperature changes, we will use the **Calculating Rate Changes** approach. We calculate the change in temperature ($\Delta T$) over equal time intervals (1 minute) to see if the heating rate is constant, increasing, or decreasing.

steps.title: Calculate initial rate
steps.content:
First, determine the temperature rise during the first minute ($t=0$ to $t=1$). Reading from the graph:

- At $t=0$ min, the line is on the second small tick above 20: $$T_0 = 24^\circ\text{C}$$
- At $t=1$ min, the line is halfway between 30 and 32: $$T_1 \approx 31^\circ\text{C}$$

Calculate the change:
$$\Delta T_{\text{initial}} = 31 - 24 = 7^\circ\text{C}$$

calculator.description: Calculate temperature change for first minute

steps.title: Calculate final rate
steps.content:
Next, determine the temperature rise during the last minute ($t=4$ to $t=5$).

- At $t=4$ min, the line is roughly: $$T_4 \approx 43^\circ\text{C}$$
- At $t=5$ min, the line is on the third small tick above 40: $$T_5 = 46^\circ\text{C}$$

Calculate the change:
$$\Delta T_{\text{final}} = 46 - 43 = 3^\circ\text{C}$$

calculator.description: Calculate temperature change for last minute

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Compare the rate of temperature increase between the start and end of the experiment:
$$7^\circ\text{C} > 3^\circ\text{C}$$

The temperature is always increasing (positive slope), but the amount it increases per minute is getting smaller. Therefore, the temperature increases at a **decreasing rate**.

2 marks total

methods.label: Direct Calculation using Voltage and Resistance

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To calculate the power supplied, we first need to extract the electrical properties of the heater from the question. We are given the constant voltage across the coil and its electrical resistance.

*   Voltage ($V$) = $9.0\text{ V}$
*   Resistance ($R$) = $12\text{ }\Omega$

highlights.text: heater operates at a constant voltage of 9.0 V

steps.title: Select the power formula
steps.content:
Since we have both voltage and resistance, the most direct way to find power is using the electrical power formula related to **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**:

$$P = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

This formula allows us to calculate the power ($P$) in Watts ($W$) directly from the known supply voltage and the load resistance.

steps.title: Calculate the power
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} P &= \frac{9.0^2}{12} \\ P &= \frac{81}{12} \\ P &= 6.75 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct substitution and one mark for the final value with the correct units.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression 9.0 squared divided by 12.

calculator.description: Calculate the power using the square of the voltage divided by the resistance.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The power supplied by the heater is **$6.75\text{ W}$**.

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
To calculate the power supplied to the heater, we first identify the given electrical parameters from the question:
- Voltage ($V$) = $9.0$ V
- Resistance ($R$) = $12 \Omega$

These concepts are part of **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

steps.title: Calculate the current
steps.content:
First, we use Ohm's Law to find the current ($I$) flowing through the heating coil. Ohm's Law is defined as:

$$V = IR$$

Rearranging for current, we get:

$$I = \frac{V}{R}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$I = \frac{9.0}{12}$$

$$I = 0.75 \text{ A}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the current (I = V/R)

steps.title: Calculate the power
steps.content:
Now that we have the current, we can calculate the electrical power ($P$) using the fundamental power formula:

$$P = VI$$

Substituting our values:

$$P = 9.0 \times 0.75$$

$$P = 6.75 \text{ W}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the power (P = VI)

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The power supplied by the heater is $6.75 \text{ W}$.

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
- Correctly showing a method to find power (in this case, finding current first).
- Providing the final answer with the correct unit (**W** or **J/s**).

steps.title: Calculate electrical power

Since we know the voltage ($V$) and resistance ($R$), we can calculate the electrical power ($P$) directly using the formula:

$$P = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

Substituting our values:

$$P = \frac{9.0^2}{12} = \frac{81}{12} = 6.75 \text{ W}$$

steps.title: Calculate total energy supplied
steps.content:
Now that we have the power, we can find the total energy ($E$) supplied over the time period ($t = 300 \text{ s}$). Energy is the product of power and time:

$$E = P \times t$$

$$E = 6.75 \text{ W} \times 300 \text{ s}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the multiplication gives us the total energy in Joules:

$$E = 2025 \text{ J}$$

The markscheme awards marks for the correct substitution and the final answer with units.

We need to read the initial and final temperatures from the graph to find the change in temperature ($\Delta T$).

- At time $t = 0 \text{ min}$, the initial temperature is $20.0^\circ \text{C}$.
- At time $t = 5 \text{ min}$, the final temperature is $34.5^\circ \text{C}$.

Now, we calculate the difference:

steps.content:
The specific heat capacity $c$ is given in $\text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$, so the mass must be in kilograms ($kg$). The question gives the mass in grams.

parts.markscheme:
- Suggest that heat is lost to the surroundings during heating <marks>R1</marks>

<note>Accept 'Not all electrical energy is transferred to the block'</note>

steps.title: Analyze the energy transfer assumption
steps.content:
In calorimetry experiments like this one, calculations typically assume a perfectly efficient system. This relies on the assumption that **all** the electrical energy supplied by the heater is converted directly into the internal energy of the aluminium block.

Ideally:
$$E_{electrical} = E_{block}$$

steps.title: Apply Energy Loss to Surroundings
steps.content:
Using the **Energy Loss to Surroundings** approach, we recognize that the block is not perfectly insulated. As the block heats up and its temperature exceeds that of the room, thermal energy naturally flows from the hot block to the cooler air.

This means the actual energy balance is:
$$E_{electrical} = E_{block} + E_{lost}$$

steps.title: Explain the discrepancy
steps.content: Since some energy is dissipated to the air, the amount of energy actually available to raise the temperature of the block is less than the total electrical energy supplied. This causes the experimental result (derived from the actual heating curve) to differ from the theoretical calculation.

steps.title: State the final reason
steps.content: The reason your answers differ is that **heat is lost to the surroundings** during the heating process.

highlights.text: Suggest that heat is lost to the surroundings during heating

parts.content: Comment on the shape of the temperature-time graph and what it suggests about the rate of heat loss.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the graph shows slight downward curvature from linearity <marks>R1</marks>
- Suggest this indicates increasing heat loss as temperature rises <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content:
To start, look closely at the path of the data points on the temperature-time graph. Rather than forming a perfectly straight line, the line begins to curve slightly as time progresses. 

We describe this shape as having a **downward curvature** (it is "concave down"). This means the steepness, or the **gradient**, of the line is decreasing over time.

highlights.text: State that the graph shows slight downward curvature from linearity

steps.title: Interpret the gradient
steps.content:
In a temperature-time graph, the gradient represents the **rate of temperature change** (how many degrees the block warms up per minute). 

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta t}$$

If the gradient is decreasing, it means the block is warming up more slowly at the end of the 5 minutes than it was at the start, even though the power supply (the energy coming in) remains constant.

gdc.instructions: Use the 'Trace' or 'Table' function to find the change in Y over the change in X for two different intervals.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the average slope between 0-1 minutes and 4-5 minutes from the graph points.

desmos.instructions: Observe the points (0,20), (1,28) and (4,47), (5,52). Calculate the slopes: m1 = (28-20)/1 = 8 and m2 = (52-47)/1 = 5.

calculator.description: We can verify the gradient is decreasing by comparing the slope at the start vs. the end.

steps.title: Relate to heat loss
steps.content:
Why is the block warming up more slowly? 

The heater provides a constant rate of thermal energy. However, as the block gets hotter, the temperature difference between the block and the cooler surrounding air increases. According to the principles of **Thermal Physics (Topic B.1)**, a larger temperature difference leads to a faster rate of energy transfer to the surroundings.

Since more energy is escaping as "heat loss" every second, there is less net energy available to raise the temperature of the block. This confirms that the **rate of heat loss is increasing** as the temperature rises.

highlights.text: Suggest this indicates increasing heat loss as temperature rises

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your answer should clearly state these two points:
1. The graph shows a **downward curvature** (or a decreasing gradient).
2. This suggests that the **rate of heat loss increases** as the block's temperature increases.

The left end of the bar is kept at $100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and the right end at $0\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$.

The system reaches a steady state in which a constant rate of thermal energy flows through the bar.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d4igLRZAGsrnoexWW-VPQ.jpeg)

parts.content: State what is meant by steady-state thermal conduction.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the temperature at any point in the bar does not change with time <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content: In thermal physics, specifically in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**, we study how heat moves through materials. The question asks for the definition of 'steady state' in the context of thermal conduction through this composite bar.

steps.title: Temperature constancy over time
steps.content:
When we first connect the ends of the bar to the $100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and $0\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ sources, the temperatures inside the blocks begin to change. However, once the system reaches a **steady state**, these temperatures stop changing.

At this stage, if you were to pick any specific point along the bar, its temperature would remain constant and not fluctuate as time passes. While different points along the bar will have different temperatures (forming a gradient), the temperature at each specific point is fixed.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam for this definition, you should focus on the time-independence of the temperature.

**Definition:** Steady-state thermal conduction is a state where the temperature at any point in the material does not change with time.

parts.content: Explain why the thermal energy flow rate is the same through all three blocks.
parts.markscheme:
- State that no accumulation of energy occurs at any point in the bar <marks>R1</marks>

- Conclude that thermal energy enters and leaves each block at the same rate <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the steady state
steps.content: The question states that the system reaches a **steady state**. This means that the temperature at every point along the bar has stabilized and is constant over time (even though the temperature varies along the length of the bar).

steps.title: Consider internal energy
steps.content: Since the temperature of the blocks is not changing, the internal energy within any section of the bar remains constant. This implies that there is **no accumulation** (or loss) of thermal energy inside the blocks.

steps.title: Apply conservation of energy
steps.content:
According to the conservation of energy, if energy is not accumulating inside a block, the rate at which energy enters must equal the rate at which it leaves. 

Therefore, the thermal energy flow rate (power) must be the same through all three blocks to maintain this balance.

parts.content:
Let $T_1$ be the temperature at the junction between block 1 and block 2, and $T_2$ at the junction between block 2 and block 3.

By equating energy transfer rates, deduce two equations involving $T_1$, $T_2$, and $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that $P = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ and is constant throughout <marks>M1</marks>

- Deduce the equation for the first junction: $\frac{k(100 - T_1)}{L} = \frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Deduce the equation for the second junction: $\frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{3kT_2}{L}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: State steady state condition
steps.content:
In a steady state, the rate of thermal energy transfer $P$ flowing through the composite bar must be constant. This means the rate of energy leaving one block must equal the rate of energy entering the next block. We can write this condition as:

$$P_1 = P_2 = P_3$$

highlights.text: constant rate of thermal energy flows

steps.title: Apply conduction equation
steps.content:
We use the thermal conduction equation to describe the rate of energy transfer through each block:

$$P = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$$

*   $k$ is the thermal conductivity
*   $A$ is the cross-sectional area (equal for all blocks)
*   $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference across the block
*   $L$ is the length of the block (equal for all blocks)

highlights.text: three blocks of equal length L

steps.title: Formulate expressions for each block
steps.content:
We can write the expression for $P$ for each block, remembering that heat flows from high temperature ($100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$) to low temperature ($0\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$):

*   **Block 1**: Conductivity $k$, $\Delta T = 100 - T_1$
    $$P_1 = \frac{kA(100 - T_1)}{L}$$

*   **Block 2**: Conductivity $k$, $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2$
    $$P_2 = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

*   **Block 3**: Conductivity $3k$, $\Delta T = T_2 - 0$
    $$P_3 = \frac{3kA(T_2)}{L}$$

highlights.text: thermal conductivities of the three blocks are k, k, and 3k

steps.title: Deduce first equation
steps.content:
Equate the rate of transfer from Block 1 to Block 2 ($P_1 = P_2$). The area $A$ is common to both sides and can be cancelled:

$$\frac{kA(100 - T_1)}{L} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

Simplifying by removing $A$ gives the first required equation:

$$\frac{k(100 - T_1)}{L} = \frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

steps.title: Deduce second equation
steps.content:
Equate the rate of transfer from Block 2 to Block 3 ($P_2 = P_3$). Again, cancel the common area $A$:

$$\frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{3kAT_2}{L}$$

Simplifying by removing $A$ gives the second required equation:

$$\frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{3kT_2}{L}$$

parts.content: Determine the values of $T_1$ and $T_2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to solve the system of equations <marks>M1</marks>

- From equation (1): $100 - T_1 = T_1 - T_2 \Rightarrow 100 = 2T_1 - T_2$
- From equation (2): $T_1 - T_2 = 3T_2 \Rightarrow T_1 = 4T_2$

- Substitute $T_1 = 4T_2$ into the first equation:
  $100 = 2(4T_2) - T_2 = 7T_2$

- Calculate $T_2$: $T_2 = \frac{100}{7} \approx 14.3\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate $T_1$: $T_1 = \frac{400}{7} \approx 57.1\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define heat flow equations
steps.content:
In steady state, the rate of heat flow (power $P$) is the same through each block. We use the fundamental definition of thermal conduction:

$$P = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$$

Applying this to each block from left to right:

- Block 1 ($100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ to $T_1$): $P = \frac{kA(100 - T_1)}{L}$
- Block 2 ($T_1$ to $T_2$): $P = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$
- Block 3 ($T_2$ to $0\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$): $P = \frac{3kA(T_2 - 0)}{L}$

highlights.text: equal length L and cross-sectional area A
left end of the bar is kept at 100

steps.title: Equate and simplify power
steps.content:
Since $P$ is constant and all blocks have the same $L$ and $A$, the factor $\frac{A}{L}$ cancels out when we equate the power through different blocks. This gives us a system of linear equations:

1. From Block 1 and Block 2:
$$k(100 - T_1) = k(T_1 - T_2) \Rightarrow 100 - T_1 = T_1 - T_2$$

2. From Block 2 and Block 3:
$$k(T_1 - T_2) = 3k(T_2) \Rightarrow T_1 - T_2 = 3T_2$$

Note: If you are unfamiliar with these mechanisms, review **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

highlights.text: thermal conductivities of the three blocks are k, k, and 3k

steps.title: Solve the system
steps.content:
Now we solve the system of equations for $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 100 &= 2T_1 - T_2 & (1) \\ T_1 &= 4T_2 & (2) \end{aligned}$$

Substituting equation (2) into (1):

$$\begin{aligned} 100 &= 2(4T_2) - T_2 \\ 100 &= 8T_2 - T_2 \\ 100 &= 7T_2 \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $T_2$:
$$T_2 = \frac{100}{7} \approx 14.3\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

Solving for $T_1$:
$$T_1 = 4 \times \frac{100}{7} = \frac{400}{7} \approx 57.1\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content:
You can quickly solve systems of linear equations like this using your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). First, rearrange the equations into the form $ax + by = c$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2T_1 - 1T_2 &= 100 \\ 1T_1 - 4T_2 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

1. Go to 'Equation' or 'System' mode.
2. Choose 'Simultaneous' and select 2 unknowns.
3. Enter the coefficients:
   a1=2, b1=-1, c1=100
   a2=1, b2=-4, c2=0
4. Press 'Solve'.

1. Press [APPS] and select 'PlySmlt2'.
2. Select '2: Simultaneous Eqn Solver'.
3. Set equations to 2 and variables to 2. Press [NEXT].
4. Enter the coefficients into the matrix:

   Row 2: [1, -4, 0]
5. Press [SOLVE] (Graph button).

desmos.instructions: Type the equations into two separate lines and find the intersection point ($T_1$, $T_2$).

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations using the simultaneous equation solver.

steps.title: Define thermal resistance
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Thermal Resistance Analogy**, we treat the flow of heat like electrical current. The thermal resistance $R$ of a material is defined by its length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and thermal conductivity $k$:

$$R = \frac{L}{kA}$$

Let's define the resistance of the first two blocks ($k_1 = k$ and $k_2 = k$) as $R$. 

For the third block ($k_3 = 3k$):
$$R_3 = \frac{L}{3kA} = \frac{1}{3}R$$

So we have three resistors in series: $R$, $R$, and $\frac{1}{3}R$.

highlights.text: thermal conductivities of the three blocks are k, k, and 3k

steps.title: Calculate total resistance
steps.content:
Since the blocks are in series, the total thermal resistance $R_{\text{total}}$ is the sum of the individual resistances:

$$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{total}} &= R_1 + R_2 + R_3 \\ R_{\text{total}} &= R + R + \frac{1}{3}R \\ R_{\text{total}} &= \frac{7}{3}R \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Use potential divider logic
steps.content:
In a steady state, the temperature drop across each block is proportional to its resistance, just like the voltage drop across resistors in series. This is often covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

The total temperature difference is:
$$\Delta T_{\text{total}} = 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 0\,^\circ\mathrm{C} = 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

The temperature drop across any part is given by:
$$\Delta T = \frac{R_{\text{part}}}{R_{\text{total}}} \times \Delta T_{\text{total}}$$

highlights.text: left end of the bar is kept at 100 ^\circ C and the right end at 0 ^\circ C

steps.title: Determine T1
steps.content:
$T_1$ is the temperature after the first block. The temperature drop across the first block is:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_1 &= \frac{R}{\frac{7}{3}R} \times 100 \\ \Delta T_1 &= \frac{3}{7} \times 100 = \frac{300}{7} \,^\circ\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

Now, calculate $T_1$:
$$T_1 = 100 - \frac{300}{7} = \frac{700 - 300}{7} = \frac{400}{7} \,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$T_1 \approx 57.1\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

calculator.description: Calculate 400 divided by 7

steps.title: Determine T2
steps.content:
$T_2$ is the temperature after the second block. We can calculate it by looking at the resistance of the final block, which is between $T_2$ and $0\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$.

The temperature drop across the last block ($R_3$) is:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_3 &= T_2 - 0 = \frac{\frac{1}{3}R}{\frac{7}{3}R} \times 100 \\ T_2 &= \frac{1}{7} \times 100 \\ T_2 &= \frac{100}{7} \,^\circ\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

$$T_2 \approx 14.3\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

calculator.description: Calculate 100 divided by 7

steps.title: Establish the proportional relationship
steps.content:
In a steady state, the rate of heat flow through each block is the same. The formula for thermal conduction is related to **Topic B.1.3: Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer**. 

Since the length $L$ and area $A$ are identical for all three blocks, the temperature drop $\Delta T$ across each block is inversely proportional to its thermal conductivity $k$:

$$\Delta T \propto \frac{1}{k}$$

highlights.text: equal length L and cross-sectional area A
steady state in which a constant rate of thermal energy flows

steps.title: Determine the drop ratio
steps.content:
The conductivities are $k$, $k$, and $3k$. Let the temperature drops across the blocks be $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$, and $\Delta T_3$. Using the inverse relationship:

$$\Delta T_1 : \Delta T_2 : \Delta T_3 = \frac{1}{k} : \frac{1}{k} : \frac{1}{3k}$$

To simplify, multiply the entire ratio by $3k$:

$$\Delta T_1 : \Delta T_2 : \Delta T_3 = 3 : 3 : 1$$

highlights.text: thermal conductivities of the three blocks are k, k, and 3k

steps.title: Partition the total temperature drop
steps.content:
The total temperature difference across the entire bar is $100\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 0\,^\circ\mathrm{C} = 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$. 

The total number of 'parts' in our ratio is:

$$3 + 3 + 1 = 7$$

Each 'part' represents a temperature drop of:

$$\text{one part} = \frac{100}{7}\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the value of one ratio part.

highlights.text: left end of the bar is kept at 100 °C and the right end at 0 °C

steps.title: Calculate T1 and T2
steps.content:
$T_1$ is the temperature after the first drop (3 parts), and $T_2$ is the temperature after the second drop (another 3 parts).

$$\begin{aligned} T_1 &= 100 - 3 \times \left(\frac{100}{7}\right) = 100 - \frac{300}{7} = \frac{400}{7} \approx 57.1\,^\circ\mathrm{C} \\ T_2 &= T_1 - 3 \times \left(\frac{100}{7}\right) = \frac{400}{7} - \frac{300}{7} = \frac{100}{7} \approx 14.3\,^\circ\mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the final drop is $T_2 - 0 = \frac{100}{7}$, which correctly represents the final 1 part of the ratio.

ti84.instructions: To find T1, calculate 400/7. To find T2, calculate 100/7.

calculator.description: Calculate decimal values for T1 and T2.

questions.specification: In an experiment to measure the specific heat capacity of a metal, a piece of metal is placed inside a container of boiling water at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The metal is then transferred into a calorimeter containing water at a temperature of $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The final equilibrium temperature of the water was measured. One source of error in this experiment is that a small mass of boiling water will be transferred to the calorimeter along with the metal.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1 Understanding thermal energy transfer mechanisms
