labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Determining specific heat capacity relies on conservation of energy (heat lost by hot objects equals heat gained by cooler water/calorimeter), and the error breaks the assumed energy balance.

parts.content: Suggest and explain the effect of the error on the measured value of the specific heat capacity of the metal.
parts.markscheme:
- The actual specific heat capacity of the metal will be less than calculated value <marks>[1]</marks>

- More thermal energy is transferred to the calorimeter and its contents than accounted for <marks>[1]</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Do not allow a bald answer.</note>

steps.title: Understand the calorimetry principle
steps.content:
In this experiment, we use the principle of energy conservation. We assume that the thermal energy lost by the metal block is equal to the thermal energy gained by the water in the calorimeter. This is governed by the formula for thermal energy transfer:

$$\text{Data Booklet: } Q = mc\Delta T$$

To find the specific heat capacity ($c$) of the metal, we rearrange the formula:

$$c_{\text{metal}} = \frac{Q_{\text{gained}}}{m_{\text{metal}} \Delta T_{\text{metal}}}$$

steps.title: Analyze the additional energy
steps.content:
The question states that a small mass of **boiling water** at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ is transferred along with the metal. 

This boiling water carries its own thermal energy into the calorimeter. Because it is at a much higher temperature than the calorimeter water ($10^{\circ}\text{C}$), it will release extra energy as it cools down to the final equilibrium temperature. 

This means the total thermal energy ($Q$) transferred to the calorimeter system is actually **greater** than the energy provided by the metal alone.

highlights.text: small mass of boiling water will be transferred to the calorimeter along with the metal

steps.title: Effect on experimental measurement
steps.content:
Because of this extra energy from the boiling water, the final equilibrium temperature of the calorimeter will be **higher** than it would have been if only the metal were transferred.

In the calculation, the experimenter assumes that all the energy gained by the calorimeter water ($Q_{\text{gained}}$) came strictly from the metal. Since $Q_{\text{gained}}$ is now larger than expected, the numerator in our equation for $c_{\text{metal}}$ becomes artificially high.

steps.title: Determine the final effect
steps.content:
Since the calculated thermal energy transfer is overestimated, the resulting value for the specific heat capacity of the metal will also be **overestimated** (higher than the true value).

Therefore, the **actual** specific heat capacity of the metal will be **less** than the calculated value.

steps.title: Identify the calorimetry equation
steps.content:
To understand how this error affects our result, we first look at the fundamental formula for thermal energy transfer from **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers** in your data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= mc\Delta T \end{aligned}$$

When we calculate the specific heat capacity ($c$) of the metal, we rearrange this to:

$$\begin{aligned} c &= \frac{Q}{m\Delta T} \end{aligned}$$

In this experiment, $Q$ is the thermal energy gained by the water in the calorimeter, $m$ is the mass of the metal, and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change of the metal.

steps.title: Analyze the energy transfer
steps.content:
The question states that a small mass of **boiling water** is transferred along with the metal. This boiling water is at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is much hotter than the water already in the calorimeter ($10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$).

Because this extra water also loses energy to the calorimeter, the total thermal energy transferred to the calorimeter water is greater than the energy lost by the metal alone. This causes the final equilibrium temperature to be **higher** than it would have been if only the metal was transferred.

highlights.text: small mass of boiling water will be transferred to the calorimeter along with the metal

steps.title: Impact on the calculation
steps.content:
Let's look at how a higher final temperature affects our formula $$c = \frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$$:

1.  **Numerator ($Q$):** We calculate $Q$ based on the temperature rise of the water in the calorimeter. Since the final temperature is higher, the measured temperature rise is larger, leading to a **calculated $Q$ that is too high**.
2.  **Denominator ($\Delta T$):** The temperature change of the metal is calculated as $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} - \text{final temperature}$. Because the final temperature is higher, the **calculated $\Delta T$ for the metal is too small**.

steps.title: Determine the final effect
steps.content:
When we divide a larger numerator ($Q$) by a smaller denominator ($\Delta T$), the resulting value for the specific heat capacity ($c$) increases significantly.

Therefore, the **calculated value** of the specific heat capacity will be **greater than the actual value** (or conversely, the actual value is less than the calculated value).

gdc.instructions: Compare the two ratios to see the effect of the error.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation for the 'true' value and the 'erroneous' value to compare.

desmos.instructions: Observe how increasing the numerator and decreasing the denominator increases the total value.

calculator.description: You can visualize how changing the numerator and denominator affects the result. If the true values were $Q=100$ and $\Delta T=10$, then $c=10$. If an error makes $Q=110$ and $\Delta T=9$, see how the result changes.

- Metal may not have been heated properly / metal may not all be at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
- Energy was lost to air during the transfer
- Energy may have been lost to the air through the calorimeter
- Water may not be at uniform temperature

The planet's surface has an average albedo of 0.3, meaning 30% of incoming solar radiation is reflected back into space. The atmosphere contains greenhouse gases, including carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and methane ($\text{CH}_4$), that absorb and emit infrared radiation.

parts.content: Calculate the average amount of solar radiation absorbed per square meter by the planet’s surface.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the absorbed radiation using the albedo: $\text{Absorbed radiation} = 1.2 \text{ kW/m}^2 \times (1 - 0.3) = 1.2 \times 0.7$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the final answer: $0.84 \text{ kW/m}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, list the values provided in the question. The incoming solar intensity ($I_{\text{in}}$) at the top of the atmosphere and the planet's albedo ($\alpha$) are given.

$$\begin{aligned} I_{\text{in}} &= 1.2 \text{ kW/m}^2 \\ \alpha &= 0.3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the absorbed fraction
steps.content:
Albedo represents the fraction of radiation that is **reflected**. Therefore, the fraction of radiation that is **absorbed** is the remaining portion.

This concept is part of **Topic B.2 Greenhouse effect**.

$$\text{Absorbed Fraction} = 1 - \text{albedo}$$

highlights.text: 30% of incoming solar radiation is reflected

steps.title: Calculate absorbed radiation
steps.content:
Multiply the incoming intensity by the absorbed fraction $(1 - \alpha)$ to find the absorbed radiation ($I_{\text{abs}}$).

$$\begin{aligned} I_{\text{abs}} &= I_{\text{in}} \times (1 - \alpha) \\ I_{\text{abs}} &= 1.2 \times (1 - 0.3) \\ &= 1.2 \times 0.7 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final absorbed intensity

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the calculation yields the final answer in $\text{kW/m}^2$.

$$I_{\text{abs}} = 0.84 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, let's identify the information provided in the question. We are given the incoming solar radiation intensity and the planet's albedo.

*   **Total Incoming Intensity ($I_{\text{in}}$):** $$1.2 \text{ kW/m}^2$$
*   **Albedo ($\{\alpha\}$):** $$0.3$$

highlights.text: average solar radiation intensity of $1.2 \text{ kW/m}^2$

steps.title: Understand the method
steps.content:
We will use the **Subtracting Reflected Radiation** approach. 

Albedo represents the fraction of radiation that is reflected back into space. The Principle of Conservation of Energy tells us that any radiation *not* reflected must be absorbed by the surface.

Our strategy is:
1.  Calculate the **reflected** radiation.
2.  Subtract the reflected amount from the **total** incoming radiation to find what remains (the absorbed radiation).

This concept relates to **Topic B.2: Greenhouse effect**.

steps.title: Calculate reflected radiation
steps.content:
First, we determine how much energy is reflected away. We multiply the total incoming intensity by the albedo ratio.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Reflected Radiation} &= I_{\text{in}} \times \text{Albedo} \\ &= 1.2 \text{ kW/m}^2 \times 0.3 \\ &= 0.36 \text{ kW/m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate reflected radiation

steps.title: Calculate absorbed radiation
steps.content:
Now, we subtract the reflected radiation from the total incoming radiation to find the absorbed portion.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Absorbed Radiation} &= I_{\text{in}} - \text{Reflected Radiation} \\ &= 1.2 \text{ kW/m}^2 - 0.36 \text{ kW/m}^2 \\ &= 0.84 \text{ kW/m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Subtract reflected radiation from total

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The average solar radiation absorbed by the planet's surface is **0.84 kW/m²**.

This matches the markscheme requirement to calculate absorbed radiation using the albedo.

parts.content: Describe how the greenhouse gases in the atmosphere influence the conservation of energy within the planet's atmosphere and surface system.
parts.markscheme:
- State the principle of energy balance: The energy absorbed by the planet's surface and atmosphere must equal the energy radiated back to space <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain the absorption by greenhouse gases: Greenhouse gases absorb outgoing infrared radiation, trapping heat in the atmosphere <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain the re-emission process: Greenhouse gases re-emit radiation back toward the Earth's surface, maintaining a higher equilibrium temperature <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award marks for clear understanding of energy conservation and greenhouse gas mechanism. All three points must link to energy conservation concept.</note>

steps.title: Global Energy Balance Principle
steps.content:
To satisfy the **Conservation of Energy**, the entire planet system must be in thermal equilibrium. This means the total power entering the system from the Sun must equal the total power radiated back out into space.

$$P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$$

If this balance weren't met, the planet would continuously heat up or cool down. The presence of an atmosphere doesn't change this global requirement; it changes *how* the energy is distributed internally.

steps.title: Nature of Radiation
steps.content:
Solar radiation arrives primarily as short-wavelength energy (visible and UV), which largely passes through the atmosphere and is absorbed by the planet's surface. 

The warmed surface then re-radiates this energy, but because the surface is much cooler than the Sun, it emits long-wavelength **infrared radiation**.

steps.title: Absorption by Greenhouse Gases
steps.content: This is where the atmosphere plays a critical role. The question states that gases like carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) and methane ($\text{CH}_4$) are present. These molecules resonate with and **absorb** the outgoing infrared radiation emitted by the surface, preventing it from escaping directly into space.

steps.title: Re-radiation Mechanism
steps.content:
Once these gas molecules absorb the energy, they become excited and re-emit the radiation in all directions—including back down toward the surface. 

This means the surface receives energy from **two sources**:
1. Incoming solar radiation
2. Re-radiated infrared from the atmosphere

This "recycling" of energy is the core of the greenhouse effect.

steps.title: New Equilibrium Temperature
steps.content:
To maintain conservation of energy at the surface, the surface must emit more energy to balance this extra input from the atmosphere. Since the rate of emission depends on temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann law), the **surface temperature increases** to a new, higher equilibrium value.

Summary for marks:
1.  **Balance:** Energy In = Energy Out for the whole system.
2.  **Absorption:** GHGs absorb outgoing IR.
3.  **Re-emission:** GHGs radiate energy back to the surface, maintaining a higher temperature.

parts.content: Explain how an increase in greenhouse gas concentrations can lead to an enhanced greenhouse effect, and discuss one possible consequence of this effect on the planet's climate.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain the effect of increased concentrations: Higher concentrations of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{CH}_4$ lead to more absorption and re-emission of infrared radiation, trapping more heat <marks>A1</marks>

- Link to temperature rise: Increased heat trapping raises the planet's average surface temperature (global warming) <marks>A1</marks>

- Discuss a consequence: For example, the melting of polar ice caps decreases the planet's albedo, which accelerates warming through a positive feedback loop <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept other valid consequences with clear explanation of mechanism.</note>

steps.title: Mechanism of interaction
steps.content: First, we must establish how greenhouse gases behave. The question states that the atmosphere contains gases like $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{CH}_4$ that interact with radiation. The planet's surface emits energy as long-wave **infrared radiation**. These greenhouse gases absorb this outgoing infrared radiation and re-emit it in all directions, sending some of it back towards the surface.

steps.title: The enhanced greenhouse effect
steps.content: When the concentration of these gases increases, the rate of absorption and re-emission increases. This means more infrared radiation is redirected back to the surface rather than escaping into space. This process traps more thermal energy in the atmosphere, creating an "enhanced" greenhouse effect.

steps.title: Link to temperature rise
steps.content: This additional trapped energy upsets the planet's thermal equilibrium. To radiate enough energy back to space to balance the incoming solar radiation, the planet's surface must get hotter. Therefore, the average surface temperature rises, leading to global warming.

steps.title: Consequence: Melting ice caps
steps.content: A critical consequence of this warming is the melting of polar ice caps. Ice is highly reflective (high albedo). As it melts, it reveals darker land or water underneath, which has a lower albedo. This darker surface absorbs more solar radiation instead of reflecting it, causing the temperature to rise even further. This is known as a **positive feedback loop**.

parts.content: Assuming no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings, state the principle of conservation of energy for this system and derive an expression for the specific heat capacity of the block, $c_b$.
parts.markscheme:
- State that thermal energy lost by the metal block equals thermal energy gained by the water: 
$$
Q_{lost} = Q_{gained}
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- Substitute the specific heat capacity formula into the balance:
$$
m_b c_b (T_b - T_f) = m_w c_w (T_f - T_w)
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- Rearrange the equation to solve for $c_b$:
$$
c_b = \frac{m_w c_w (T_f - T_w)}{m_b (T_b - T_f)}
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the specific heat capacity of the metal block using the values provided in the table.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the energy gained by the water:
$$
Q_w = 0.250 \times 4180 \times (24.8 - 21.5) = 3448.5 \text{ J}
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- Substitute values into the derived expression for $c_b$:
$$
c_b = \frac{3448.5}{0.120 \times (100.0 - 24.8)}
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

- Final calculation and unit:
$$
c_b = \frac{3448.5}{9.024} \approx 382 \mathrm{ \ J\,kg^{-1}\,K^{-1}}
$$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how conduction and convection are involved in heating the water inside the saucepan.
parts.markscheme:
Conduction in the saucepan:
- Heat is transferred from the stove to the base of the metal saucepan by conduction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In metals, thermal energy is transferred through the vibrations and collisions of atoms as well as the movement of free electrons. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Convection in the water:
- Once the base of the saucepan is hot, heat is transferred to the water through conduction. As the water at the bottom heats up, it becomes less dense and rises, while cooler, denser water sinks. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This circulation of warm and cool water sets up convection currents, which transfer heat throughout the water. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the physical setup
steps.content:
We need to trace the thermal energy transfer chronologically, starting from the stove, moving through the **metal saucepan**, and finally distributing throughout the water. This involves two distinct mechanisms: conduction and convection.

This relates to **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

steps.title: Conduction through the metal pan
steps.content:
First, thermal energy is transferred from the stove to the base of the saucepan. Since the pan is solid metal, the mechanism is **conduction**.

In metals, this happens in two specific ways:
1. **Lattice vibrations**: Atoms vibrate more vigorously and collide with neighbors.
2. **Free electrons**: Mobile electrons gain kinetic energy and move rapidly through the metal, colliding with other atoms and transferring energy efficiently.

This explains why the metal heats up quickly.

highlights.text: Heat is transferred from the stove to the base of the metal saucepan by conduction.

steps.title: Heating the bottom water layer
steps.content: Once the inside bottom surface of the pan is hot, it transfers thermal energy to the immediate layer of water molecules touching it. This initial transfer from solid to liquid occurs via **conduction**.

steps.title: Density changes in water
steps.content:
As the water at the bottom absorbs heat:
1. The molecules move faster and move slightly further apart.
2. The volume of this water expands.
3. Consequently, the **density decreases** (it becomes lighter than the surrounding cooler water).

highlights.text: As the water at the bottom heats up, it becomes less dense

steps.title: Formation of convection currents
steps.content:
Because the hot water is less dense, it rises to the top. The cooler, denser water at the top sinks to the bottom to replace it.

This continuous cycle of rising warm water and sinking cool water establishes **convection currents**, which distribute thermal energy throughout the entire volume of water.

parts.content: Describe how radiation plays a role in this heating process and how it differs from conduction and convection.
parts.markscheme:
Role of radiation: Radiation occurs when thermal energy is emitted from a hot surface as infrared radiation, which can travel through the surrounding air to heat nearby objects. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Difference from conduction: Unlike conduction, radiation does not require direct contact between objects; it can occur even through a vacuum. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Difference from convection: Unlike convection, radiation does not rely on fluid movement. Radiation can transfer heat in all directions without requiring a medium like air or water. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the key concepts
steps.content:
This question asks us to explain the mechanism of **thermal radiation** and distinguish it from conduction and convection. These concepts are covered in **Topic B.1 Thermal energy transfers**.

We need to focus on three distinct points for the 3 marks:
1. What radiation is (mechanism).
2. How it differs from conduction (contact).
3. How it differs from convection (medium).

steps.title: Define thermal radiation
steps.content:
Radiation is the transfer of thermal energy through the emission of **infrared electromagnetic waves** from a hot source. 

In this scenario, the hot stove element and the heated saucepan emit invisible infrared waves into the surrounding environment, transferring energy to nearby objects without physically touching them.

highlights.text: emitted from a hot surface as infrared radiation

steps.title: Contrast with conduction
steps.content:
The primary difference between radiation and conduction is the requirement for **physical contact**.

*   **Conduction** transfers energy through particle collisions and vibrations within a material (e.g., heat traveling from the bottom of the pan to the handle). It requires the objects to touch.
*   **Radiation** does **not** require direct contact. The heat you feel on your hand when placed *near* (but not touching) the hot stove is due to radiation.

steps.title: Contrast with convection
steps.content:
The primary difference between radiation and convection is the requirement for a **medium**.

*   **Convection** transfers heat through the bulk movement of fluids (liquids or gases), such as hot water rising and cold water sinking in the saucepan.
*   **Radiation** does **not** rely on the movement of matter. It can travel through air and even through a vacuum (empty space).

highlights.text: does not rely on fluid movement

steps.title: Summarize the answer
steps.content:
To earn the full 3 marks, your answer should explicitly state:

1.  **Mechanism:** Heat is transferred via the emission of **infrared waves**.
2.  **Vs Conduction:** Unlike conduction, radiation does not need **direct contact**.
3.  **Vs Convection:** Unlike convection, radiation does not need a **medium** (it works in a vacuum).

The table shows the properties of the radiation emitted by an ideal black body at two different absolute temperatures.

| Absolute temperature / K | Peak emission wavelength / m | Total power radiated / W |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T$ | $\lambda_{max}$ | $P$ |
| $3T$ | $W$ | $X$ |

Which of the following correctly identifies the values for $W$ and $X$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.1 Thermal energy transfers > B.1.3 Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.3 Mechanisms of thermal energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses blackbody radiation relationships: Wien’s displacement law for peak wavelength (λ_max ∝ 1/T) and Stefan–Boltzmann law for total power radiated (P ∝ T^4), both within thermal radiation/heat transfer mechanisms.

options.markscheme: Using Wien’s displacement law, $\lambda_{max} \propto \frac{1}{T}$ so $W=\frac{\lambda_{max}}{3}$ is correct. Using Stefan–Boltzmann law, total power $P \propto T^4$, so at $3T$ the factor is $3^4=81$, not $9$.

Wien’s displacement law: $\lambda_{max}T=\text{constant}$ so when temperature increases from $T$ to $3T$, peak wavelength decreases by a factor of $3$: $W=\frac{\lambda_{max}}{3}$. Stefan–Boltzmann law: $P \propto T^4$, so $X=P\left(\frac{3T}{T}\right)^4=P\cdot 3^4=81P$.

Stefan–Boltzmann law gives $X=81P$ correctly, but Wien’s displacement law requires peak wavelength to decrease with increasing temperature, so $W$ should be $\frac{\lambda_{max}}{3}$, not $3\lambda_{max}$.
Both relationships are incorrect. Wien’s displacement law requires $W=\frac{\lambda_{max}}{3}$ when temperature increases to $3T$, and Stefan–Boltzmann law requires $X=81P$ since $P \propto T^4$.

parts.content: Calculate the temperature change of the substance.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the temperature change: $\Delta T = 75.0 - 25.0 = 50.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (or $50.0\ \text{K}$) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify given temperatures
steps.content:
To find the temperature change, we first need to identify the initial and final temperatures provided in the question. 

*   Initial temperature ($T_{\text{initial}}$) = $25.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
*   Final temperature ($T_{\text{final}}$) = $75.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$

steps.title: Calculate temperature difference
steps.content:
The temperature change, denoted as $\Delta T$, is calculated by subtracting the initial temperature from the final temperature:

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta T = 75.0\ ^\circ\text{C} - 25.0\ ^\circ\text{C} = 50.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 75.0 - 25.0 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the final and initial temperatures.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The temperature change is $50.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$. 

In Physics, a change of $1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\ \text{K}$, so the answer can also be expressed as $50.0\ \text{K}$. Based on the markscheme, either unit is acceptable as long as the numerical value is correct and follows significant figure rules (3 s.f.).

parts.content: Calculate the thermal energy absorbed by the substance.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the formula $Q = mc\Delta T$: $Q = 0.30 \times 850 \times 50.0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final energy value: $Q = 12750\ \text{J}$ (accept $1.28 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$ or $1.3 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To calculate the thermal energy absorbed, we first need to extract the known values from the question. We are given the mass of the substance, its specific heat capacity, and the temperature range over which it is heated.

*   Mass ($m$) = $0.30\ \text{kg}$
*   Specific heat capacity ($c$) = $850\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$
*   Initial temperature ($T_1$) = $25.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
*   Final temperature ($T_2$) = $75.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$

steps.title: Calculate the temperature change
steps.content:
The formula for thermal energy depends on the change in temperature ($\Delta T$). Note that a change in temperature of $1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is equivalent to a change of $1\ \text{K}$.

$$\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$$
$$\Delta T = 75.0\ ^\circ\text{C} - 25.0\ ^\circ\text{C} = 50.0\ \text{K}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the difference in temperature.

steps.title: Apply specific heat formula
steps.content:
We use the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of the data booklet to find the energy ($Q$).

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Substituting our known values:
$$Q = 0.30 \times 850 \times 50.0$$

steps.title: Calculate final energy value
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us the total thermal energy absorbed in Joules (J).

$$Q = 12750\ \text{J}$$

In an IB exam, you can express this as $1.28 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$ (rounded to 3 significant figures) or $1.3 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$ (rounded to 2 significant figures).

parts.content: Determine the time taken for this energy transfer.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the formula $t = \frac{Q}{P}$: $t = \frac{12750}{200.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the time taken: $t = 63.75\ \text{s}$ (accept $63.8\ \text{s}$ or $64\ \text{s}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the time taken, we first need to identify the variables provided in the question:

*   Mass ($m$) = $0.30\ \text{kg}$
*   Initial temperature ($T_1$) = $25.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
*   Final temperature ($T_2$) = $75.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$
*   Specific heat capacity ($c$) = $850\ \text{J kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
*   Power ($P$) = $200.0\ \text{W}$

steps.title: Calculate temperature change
steps.content:
First, we calculate the change in temperature ($\Delta T$). Since a change of $1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is equal to a change of $1\ \text{K}$, we can use the values directly:

$$\Delta T = 75.0\ ^\circ\text{C} - 25.0\ ^\circ\text{C} = 50.0\ \text{K}$$

calculator.description: Subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature.

steps.title: Calculate total thermal energy
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the total thermal energy ($Q$) required to heat the sample using the formula from the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Substituting our values:

$$Q = 0.30 \times 850 \times 50.0 = 12750\ \text{J}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy transferred.

steps.title: Determine time taken
steps.content:
Power ($P$) is defined as the rate of energy transfer over time ($t$). This relationship is given by:

$$P = \frac{Q}{t}$$

Rearranging to solve for time:

$$t = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{12750}{200.0} = 63.75\ \text{s}$$

According to the markscheme, $63.75\ \text{s}$ is the exact answer, though rounded values like $63.8\ \text{s}$ or $64\ \text{s}$ are also acceptable. This question uses concepts from **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers** and **Topic A.3: Power**.

calculator.description: Divide the total energy by the power of the heater.

steps.title: Connect energy and power
steps.content:
To find the time, we need to link the thermal energy required to heat the substance with the power supplied by the heater. From the IB Data Booklet, we use the thermal energy formula and the definition of power:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= mc\Delta T \\ P &= \frac{Q}{t} \end{aligned}$$

Here, $Q$ is the energy, $m$ is the mass, $c$ is the specific heat capacity, $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature, $P$ is power, and $t$ is time. These concepts are found in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers** and **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Rearrange for time
steps.content:
Using the relationship $Q = P \times t$, we can combine the two equations into one and rearrange it to make $t$ the subject:

$$\begin{aligned} P \times t &= mc\Delta T \\ t &= \frac{mc\Delta T}{P} \end{aligned}$$

We know that the temperature change $\Delta T$ is the difference between the final and initial temperatures ($T_{final} - T_{initial}$).

steps.title: Direct substitution and calculation
steps.content:
Now we substitute all the values from the question into our rearranged formula:
- $m = 0.30\text{ kg}$
- $c = 850\text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- $\Delta T = (75.0 - 25.0) = 50.0\text{ K}$
- $P = 200.0\text{ W}$

$$t = \frac{0.30 \times 850 \times (75.0 - 25.0)}{200.0}$$

$$t = 63.75\text{ s}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the time directly using the substituted values.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, the time taken for this energy transfer is $63.75\text{ s}$. You can also round this to three significant figures as $63.8\text{ s}$ or two significant figures as $64\text{ s}$.

1 mark is awarded for the correct substitution (M1) and 1 mark for the final numerical value (A1).

steps.title: Define the rate method
steps.content:
To find the time taken using the **Rate of Temperature Increase** approach, we first look at the relationship between power, mass, and specific heat. From the data booklet, we know the energy required is given by:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Since power ($P$) is the rate of energy transfer ($P = \frac{Q}{t}$), we can substitute $Q$ to find the rate at which the temperature rises:

$$P = mc \times \left(\frac{\Delta T}{t}\right)$$

Rearranging this, the rate of temperature increase (degrees per second) is:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{P}{mc}$$

steps.title: Calculate the temperature rate
steps.content:
Using the values provided for power, mass, and specific heat capacity, we calculate the rate of temperature increase.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Rate} &= \frac{200.0}{0.30 \times 850} \\ &= \frac{200.0}{255} \\ &\approx 0.7843... \text{ K s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the rate of temperature increase

steps.title: Find total temperature change
steps.content:
Next, we determine the total temperature change ($\Delta T$) required for the sample.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta T &= 75.0 - 25.0 \\ &= 50.0 \text{ } ^\circ\text{C} \text{ (or K)} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for time
steps.content:
Finally, we divide the total temperature change by the rate of increase to find the total time taken ($t = \frac{\Delta T}{\text{Rate}}$).

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{50.0}{0.7843...} \\ t &= 63.75 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, we earn one mark for substituting the correct values and one mark for the final answer of $63.75\text{ s}$.

parts.content: State one assumption made about the system in this calculation.

- State that no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings (or the heating process is 100% efficient) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze energy transfer
steps.content:
In problems involving thermal energy, we use the principle of conservation of energy. We typically equate the energy supplied by the heater ($Q$) to the energy absorbed by the substance, calculated using the formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

To make this calculation simple and direct, we have to assume that every Joule of energy provided by the heater actually reaches the sample.

steps.title: Identify the ideal condition
steps.content: In a real-world scenario, some thermal energy would escape into the air or be absorbed by the container holding the substance. However, for the purpose of the IB Physics curriculum (Topic B.1), we assume an ideal system where the energy transfer is perfectly contained within the sample.

steps.title: State the assumption
steps.content:
Based on the principle of energy conservation, the required assumption is that **no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings**. 

Alternatively, you can state that the **heating process is 100% efficient**, meaning all energy supplied by the $200.0\text{ W}$ heater is used solely to increase the temperature of the $0.30\text{ kg}$ sample.

highlights.text: State that no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings (or the heating process is 100% efficient)

steps.title: Assume constant specific heat
steps.content:
For our calculation using the thermal physics equations, we assume that the specific heat capacity ($c$) of the substance is constant and does not change across the temperature range from $25.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $75.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$. 

In real-world scenarios, the specific heat capacity can vary slightly as the temperature of a substance increases, but in the IB Physics curriculum, we treat it as a fixed value ($850\ \text{J kg}^{-1}\ \text{K}^{-1}$) to simplify the problem.

steps.title: Assume no heat loss
steps.content:
Another critical assumption required for this calculation is that the system is perfectly insulated. This means we assume that no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings or absorbed by the container. 

This ensures that 100% of the energy supplied by the heater ($P = 200.0\ \text{W}$) is transferred directly to the sample to raise its temperature, allowing us to use the equation from the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you should state that there is no energy loss to the surroundings. This is the most common answer expected by the markscheme for these types of calorimetry problems.

**Assumption:** No thermal energy is lost to the surroundings (or the process is 100% efficient).

highlights.text: State that no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings (or the heating process is 100% efficient)

steps.title: Analyze the energy transfer
steps.content:
In thermal physics problems involving heating a sample, we typically use the specific heat capacity formula from the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

In this calculation, we use the mass ($0.30\text{ kg}$) and specific heat capacity ($850\text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$) of the **substance** only. This implies that all energy provided by the heater is transferred exclusively to the substance itself.

steps.title: Assume negligible container mass
steps.content:
To simplify the calculation, we must assume that the **thermal energy absorbed by the container holding the substance or the heater itself is negligible**. 

In a real-world scenario, the beaker and the heating element would also increase in temperature, consuming some of the energy. By ignoring their mass and heat capacity, we assume that 100% of the heater's power is used to heat the sample.

steps.title: Final assumption statement
steps.content: Therefore, a valid assumption for this system is that no thermal energy is absorbed by the container (it has negligible mass/heat capacity), which aligns with the requirement that the heating process is 100% efficient.

highlights.text: State one assumption made about the system in this calculation.

parts.content: Describe how the internal energy of the substance changes during the heating.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that the internal energy increases because the average kinetic energy of the particles increases as the temperature rises <marks>A1</marks>
- State that the potential energy of the particles remains constant as there is no phase change <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define internal energy components
steps.content:
To understand how internal energy changes, we use the **Microscopic Component Analysis** approach. In the IB Physics syllabus (Topic B.1), internal energy is defined as the sum of two microscopic components:

1.  **Total random kinetic energy** of the molecules.
2.  **Total intermolecular potential energy** of the molecules.

steps.title: Analyze kinetic energy change
steps.content:
The sample is heated from $25.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $75.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$. According to the kinetic theory of matter, the average kinetic energy of the particles is directly proportional to the absolute temperature, as shown in the data booklet:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Because the temperature increases, the average (and thus total) random kinetic energy of the particles increases. This accounts for the first mark.

steps.title: Analyze potential energy change
steps.content:
Next, we look at the intermolecular potential energy. Potential energy changes significantly only during a **phase change** (like melting or boiling), where energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces rather than increase temperature. 

Since the substance is simply increasing in temperature within the same phase, the average distance between particles remains relatively constant. Therefore, the total intermolecular potential energy remains constant. This accounts for the second mark.

steps.title: Summarize total internal energy
steps.content:
Since the internal energy is the sum of these two components:

$$\text{Internal Energy} = \text{Total KE (increases)} + \text{Total PE (constant)}$$

We can conclude that the **total internal energy of the substance increases** during the heating process.

steps.title: Apply the First Law
steps.content:
To describe the change in internal energy, we can use the **First Law of Thermodynamics**. This law relates the heat energy added to a system ($Q$), the change in its internal energy ($\Delta U$), and the work done by the system ($W$):

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

In this scenario, heat is being supplied by the heater to the substance. Since the substance is simply being heated and there is no mention of it expanding or performing work, we can assume that the work done by the substance is zero ($W = 0$).

steps.title: Analyze internal energy increase
steps.content:
Since heat energy is being added ($Q > 0$) and no work is being done ($W = 0$), the First Law simplifies to:

$$\Delta U = Q$$

This means that all the thermal energy supplied by the heater goes directly into increasing the **internal energy** ($U$) of the substance. Internal energy is the sum of the random kinetic energy and the potential energy of the particles within the substance.

steps.title: Relate temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
The question states the substance is heated from $25.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $75.0\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Temperature is a macroscopic measure of the average random kinetic energy of the particles. 

From the data booklet, we know that the average kinetic energy is proportional to the absolute temperature:

$$\overline{E_{k}} = \frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

As the temperature increases, the **average kinetic energy** of the particles also increases. This accounts for the rise in internal energy.

steps.title: Consider potential energy
steps.content:
During this heating process, the substance remains in the same phase (no melting or boiling is mentioned). Potential energy in a substance is associated with the bonds and intermolecular forces between particles, which change during a phase change.

Since there is **no phase change**, the **potential energy** of the particles remains **constant**. Therefore, the total increase in internal energy is entirely due to the increase in the particles' kinetic energy.

parts.content: Explain why the water level does not change significantly after the ice has melted, using physics principles.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that a floating object displaces a weight of fluid equal to its own weight <marks>M1</marks>
- **Identify** that when the ice melts, the mass (and thus weight) of the water produced is exactly equal to the mass of the original ice <marks>A1</marks>
- **Conclude** that since the weight of the displaced water equals the weight of the meltwater, the volume of displaced water equals the volume of the meltwater, leading to no net change in level <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Apply Archimedes' Principle
steps.content:
To understand why the water level stays the same, we first look at the ice while it is still floating. For any object to float in equilibrium, the upward buoyant force must equal the downward weight of the object.

According to **Archimedes' Principle**, a floating object displaces a weight of fluid equal to its own weight. 

$$\text{Weight of displaced water} = \text{Weight of ice}$$

steps.title: Conservation of mass
steps.content:
Next, consider what happens when the ice melts. Melting is a phase change, and during this process, mass is conserved. 

The mass of the liquid water produced (the meltwater) is exactly equal to the mass of the original ice cube. Since weight is calculated as $W = mg$, the weight of this meltwater is also equal to the weight of the original ice.

$$\text{Weight of meltwater} = \text{Weight of ice}$$

steps.title: Compare volumes
steps.content:
Now we can link the two ideas:
1. From Archimedes: The weight of the water originally displaced by the ice is equal to the weight of the ice.
2. From conservation of mass: The weight of the water produced by the melting ice is equal to the weight of the ice.

This means the weight (and therefore the volume) of the meltwater is identical to the weight (and volume) of the water that was displaced. When the ice melts, the resulting liquid water perfectly fills the "hole" in the water that the ice was previously occupying. Consequently, there is no net change in the water level.

steps.title: Apply Archimedes' Principle
steps.content:
A floating object is in equilibrium, meaning the upward buoyant force (upthrust) must equal the downward weight of the object. According to Archimedes' Principle, the upthrust is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the submerged part of the object.

$$\text{Weight of ice} = \text{Weight of displaced water}$$

Using the definition of weight $W = mg$, where $m$ is mass and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity:

$$m_{\text{ice}} \cdot g = m_{\text{displaced water}} \cdot g$$

highlights.text: displaces a weight of fluid equal to its own weight

steps.title: Identify Conservation of Mass
steps.content:
When the ice cube melts, it undergoes a phase change from solid to liquid. During this process, the total number of molecules remains the same, so the mass of the water produced (the meltwater) is exactly equal to the mass of the original ice cube.

$$m_{\text{ice}} = m_{\text{meltwater}}$$

highlights.text: mass (and thus weight) of the water produced is exactly equal to the mass of the original ice

steps.title: Derive Volumes using Density
steps.content:
We can relate mass and volume using the definition of density, $\rho = \frac{m}{V}$. Rearranging for volume, we get $V = \frac{m}{\rho}$.

1. For the **displaced water**, the volume is:
$$V_{\text{displaced}} = \frac{m_{\text{displaced water}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

2. For the **meltwater**, the volume is:
$$V_{\text{meltwater}} = \frac{m_{\text{meltwater}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$

From our previous steps, we know $m_{\text{displaced water}} = m_{\text{ice}}$ and $m_{\text{meltwater}} = m_{\text{ice}}$. Therefore, the masses are equal:

$$m_{\text{displaced water}} = m_{\text{meltwater}}$$

steps.title: Conclude Water Level Stability
steps.content:
Since the masses are the same and the densities are the same (both are liquid water), the volumes must also be identical:

$$V_{\text{displaced}} = V_{\text{meltwater}}$$

Because the volume of the "hole" in the water surface (the displaced volume) is exactly filled by the volume of water produced by the melting ice, there is no net change in the water level.

highlights.text: volume of displaced water equals the volume of the meltwater

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The question asks for one way to increase the **precision** of the experiment. In physics, precision is closely linked to the **resolution** of your measuring instruments—the smallest change the instrument can detect.

steps.title: Evaluate current equipment
steps.content:
Currently, the student is taking manual visual readings from a measuring cylinder. This method has two main limitations for precision:
1. **Human Error**: Parallax error or difficulty in judging the exact meniscus position.
2. **Resolution**: The cylinder likely has large graduations (e.g., every $1\text{ ml}$ or $2\text{ ml}$), meaning the measurement uncertainty is relatively high.

steps.title: Switch to digital sensors
steps.content:
A significant improvement would be to replace manual readings with a **digital water level sensor**. 

Digital sensors can often measure changes in height much more accurately than the human eye and usually provide data to more decimal places, significantly increasing the precision of each data point.

highlights.text: using a digital water level sensor

steps.title: Increase scale resolution
steps.content:
If digital equipment isn't available, you could use a measuring cylinder with **smaller graduations** (a finer scale). 

For example, moving from a cylinder with $1\text{ cm}^3$ marks to one with $0.1\text{ cm}^3$ marks reduces the absolute uncertainty of each reading, resulting in more precise height data.

steps.title: Final Recommendation
steps.content:
To earn the mark, simply suggest one of these higher-resolution options:
- Use a digital water level sensor.
- Use a measuring cylinder with smaller/finer graduations.

highlights.text: using a digital water level sensor

steps.title: Analyze current measurement method
steps.content: To suggest an improvement, we first look at how the student is currently collecting data. The problem states that the student is recording the water height at a specific interval.

highlights.text: records the height of the water surface every 30 seconds

steps.title: Increase sampling frequency
steps.content:
A key way to improve the precision of the experimental trend is to **increase the sampling frequency**. This means taking measurements at shorter time intervals.

Instead of waiting 30 seconds between readings, the student could record the height every 10 seconds or even every 5 seconds. This is a common technique used in **Topic A.1: Kinematics** and general experimental design to capture more detail in changing systems.

steps.title: Explain the benefit
steps.content: By increasing the number of data points, we can more accurately determine the shape of the curve or the gradient of the line. More data points reduce the uncertainty in the line of best fit, making the determination of the melting rate more precise.

steps.title: Final Suggestion
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, suggest taking more frequent measurements to earn the mark.

**Final Suggestion:** Take height measurements at more frequent intervals (e.g., every 10 seconds instead of every 30 seconds).

steps.title: Analyze the goal
steps.content: The question asks for an improvement to increase the **precision** of the experiment. Precision refers to how close measurements are to each other. In this context, we want to ensure that the change in water level is solely due to the melting ice and not influenced by external "noise" from the surroundings.

highlights.text: increase the precision of this experiment

steps.title: Identify environmental factors
steps.content:
During the experiment, two main environmental factors can introduce random errors:
1. **Evaporation:** Water molecules leaving the surface as vapor, which would slowly decrease the water level independent of the melting process.
2. **Ambient temperature fluctuations:** Changes in room temperature can affect the rate of heat transfer ($Q$) to the ice, causing the melting rate to fluctuate inconsistently.

This relates to **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**, where we study how systems interact with their surroundings.

steps.title: Suggest a control method
steps.content:
To increase precision, we should **shield the container from the environment**. A simple way to do this is to place a lid on the measuring cylinder or place the entire setup inside a draft-shield.

By using a lid, we minimize water loss through evaporation and protect the system from sudden air currents that could change the temperature local to the cylinder.

steps.title: Final recommendation
steps.content:
The best improvement following this approach is to **use a lid to minimize evaporation and shield the container from ambient temperature fluctuations**. This reduces random variations in the data, leading to a smoother and more precise set of results.

According to the markscheme, this is a valid way to earn the mark.

highlights.text: ensuring the container is shielded from evaporation and temperature fluctuations

parts.content: Suppose the ice cube contains a small steel ball. Predict and explain how this would affect the water level after the ice melts.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that with the steel ball, the total weight increases, causing the floating ice to displace more water initially <marks>M1</marks>
- **Explain** that after melting, the steel ball sinks and displaces a volume of water equal only to its own volume, which is less than the volume of water it displaced while floating <marks>R1</marks>
- **Predict** that the water level will decrease after the ice has completely melted <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the floating state
steps.content:
Initially, the ice cube and the steel ball are floating as a single system. According to **Archimedes' Principle**, a floating object displaces a weight of fluid equal to its own total weight.

Because the steel ball is being supported by the ice, it contributes to the total weight of the floating system. This means that while floating, the system must displace an amount of water whose weight is equal to the weight of the ice plus the weight of the steel ball.

highlights.text: total weight increases, causing the floating ice to displace more water initially

steps.title: Compare densities and displacement
steps.content:
Let's look at the volume of water displaced specifically to support the steel ball. Since the ball is much denser than water ($ \rho_{steel} > \rho_{water} $), the volume of water required to match the ball's weight is significantly **larger** than the actual physical volume of the ball.

$$\text{Volume displaced (floating)} = \frac{m_{ball}}{\rho_{water}}$$

$$\text{Actual volume of ball} = \frac{m_{ball}}{\rho_{steel}}$$

Since $\rho_{steel} > \rho_{water}$, the volume of water displaced while floating is much greater than the ball's own volume.

steps.title: Analyze the sunken state
steps.content:
Once the ice melts, the steel ball is no longer supported and sinks to the bottom. A submerged (sunken) object only displaces a volume of water equal to its **own physical volume**.

We know from the previous step that this physical volume is much smaller than the volume of water the ball was displacing while it was being held up by the ice.

highlights.text: steel ball sinks and displaces a volume of water equal only to its own volume, which is less than the volume of water it displaced while floating

steps.title: Predict the final level
steps.content:
The water from the melted ice fills exactly the volume the ice displaced while floating (so the ice itself causes no change). However, because the steel ball now displaces **less** water than it did initially, the total volume of water displaced by the system decreases.

Therefore, the overall water level in the measuring cylinder will **decrease**.

parts.content: Calculate the thermal energy required to raise the temperature of the water.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the thermal energy formula: $Q = mc\Delta T = 0.750 \times 4200 \times (100.0 - 20.0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the thermal energy: $252000\ \text{J}$ (or $2.52 \times 10^5\ \text{J}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To calculate the energy required to change the temperature of a substance, we use the specific heat capacity formula found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your IB data booklet:

$$Q = mcΔT$$

Where:
- $Q$ is the thermal energy (J)
- $m$ is the mass (kg)
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity ($\text{J } \text{kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$)
- $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature (K or $^\circ$C)

steps.title: Calculate the temperature change
steps.content:
First, we need to find the change in temperature, $\Delta T$. The water is heated from $20.0^\circ\text{C}$ to its boiling point of $100.0^\circ\text{C}$.

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$$
$$\Delta T = 100.0 - 20.0 = 80.0^\circ\text{C}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the temperature difference.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the mass ($m = 0.750\text{ kg}$), the specific heat capacity of water ($c = 4200\text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$), and our calculated $\Delta T$ into the energy equation.

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= mc\Delta T \\ Q &= 0.750 \times 4200 \times 80.0 \\ Q &= 252000\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

In the exam, showing this substitution step is usually worth 1 mark (M1).

calculator.description: Calculate the total thermal energy.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The final answer can be written in standard notation or scientific notation. 

$$Q = 2.52 \times 10^5\text{ J}$$

This final calculation earns the second mark (A1). Remember to include the units (Joules) for a complete answer.

parts.content: Determine the electrical energy input required, accounting for the efficiency.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the efficiency formula: $E = \frac{Q}{\eta} = \frac{252000}{0.850}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the electrical energy input: $2.96 \times 10^5\ \text{J}$ (or $296000\ \text{J}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate thermal energy required
steps.content:
First, we calculate the useful thermal energy ($Q$) required to heat the water. Using the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet, we identify the mass ($m$), specific heat capacity ($c$), and the change in temperature ($\Delta T$).

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta T &= 100.0^\circ\text{C} - 20.0^\circ\text{C} = 80.0\text{ K} \\ Q &= mc\Delta T \\ Q &= 0.750 \times 4200 \times 80.0 \\ Q &= 252000\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This concept is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

desmos.instructions: Type the multiplication expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total thermal energy needed.

steps.title: Account for efficiency
steps.content:
Since the kettle is not perfectly efficient, the electrical energy input ($E_{in}$) must be greater than the thermal energy output ($Q$). We use the efficiency ($\eta$) formula from **Topic A.3.3: Power and efficiency**.

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{\text{useful energy out}}{\text{total energy in}} \\ 0.850 &= \frac{252000}{E_{in}} \\ E_{in} &= \frac{252000}{0.850} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting these values earns the first mark (M1).

steps.title: Determine final energy input
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the value and round to three significant figures to match the precision of the given data.

$$E_{in} \approx 296471\text{ J}$$

In standard form, this is:
$$E_{in} = 2.96 \times 10^5\text{ J}$$

This final calculation earns the second mark (A1).

desmos.instructions: Input the fraction to find the total energy.

calculator.description: Divide the thermal energy by the efficiency decimal.

steps.title: Calculate useful power output
steps.content:
First, we need to find how much power is actually going into heating the water. Efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful power output to total power input. We can find the useful power ($P_{useful}$) using the efficiency ($\eta$) and the kettle's power rating ($P_{total}$):

$$\begin{aligned} P_{useful} &= \eta \times P_{total} \\ P_{useful} &= 0.850 \times 1500 \text{ W} \\ P_{useful} &= 1275 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

This tells us that out of the $1500\text{ W}$ supplied, $1275\text{ J}$ of energy is transferred to the water every second.

calculator.description: Calculate the useful power output.

steps.title: Determine heating time
steps.content:
Next, we find out how long the kettle needs to run to provide the required thermal energy. From the previous part of the question, we know the thermal energy required to heat the water ($Q$) is $252,000\text{ J}$. 

Using the definition of power as energy per unit time ($P = \frac{E}{t}$), we can solve for time ($t$):

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{Q}{P_{useful}} \\ t &= \frac{252000 \text{ J}}{1275 \text{ W}} \\ t &\approx 197.65 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This is the duration the kettle must be switched on to reach the boiling point.

calculator.description: Calculate the time required for heating.

steps.title: Calculate total energy input
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the total electrical energy input ($E_{input}$). This is the total power drawn from the wall multiplied by the total time elapsed. 

$$\begin{aligned} E_{input} &= P_{total} \times t \\ E_{input} &= 1500 \text{ W} \times 197.65... \text{ s} \\ E_{input} &\approx 296470.6 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get:

$$E_{input} = 2.96 \times 10^5 \text{ J}$$

This matches the result expected by the markscheme using the efficiency formula directly ($E = \frac{Q}{\eta}$).

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy input using the exact time value.

steps.title: Calculate thermal energy required
steps.content:
First, we need to find the energy required to heat the water. This is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. We use the specific heat capacity formula from the data booklet:

$$\Delta T = 100.0 - 20.0 = 80.0^\circ\text{C}$$

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= 0.750 \times 4200 \times 80.0 \\ Q &= 252,000\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate total energy input
steps.content:
Since the kettle is only $85.0\%$ efficient, we must provide more electrical energy than the thermal energy we just calculated. The total electrical energy $E$ is found by dividing the useful thermal energy by the efficiency (as decimal $0.85$):

$$\begin{aligned} E &= \frac{Q}{\text{efficiency}} \\ E &= \frac{252,000}{0.85} \\ E &\approx 296,471\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy input by dividing the heat energy by efficiency.

steps.title: Find the operating time
steps.content:
Finally, we use the definition of power from **Topic A.3.3: Power and efficiency** to find the time. Power is the rate of energy transfer:

$$P = \frac{E}{t} \implies t = \frac{E}{P}$$

Substituting the total energy and the power rating of $1500\text{ W}$:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{296,471}{1500} \\ t &\approx 197.6\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get $198\text{ s}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the time by dividing energy by power.

steps.title: Calculate energy required
steps.content:
First, we determine the total thermal energy ($Q$) needed to heat the water. We use the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section:

$$Q = mcΔT$$

Given:
- $m = 0.750\text{ kg}$
- $c = 4200\text{ J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$
- $ΔT = 100.0^\circ\text{C} - 20.0^\circ\text{C} = 80.0^\circ\text{C}$

$$Q = 0.750 \times 4200 \times 80.0 = 252,000\text{ J}$$

gdc.instructions: Input 0.75 * 4200 * 80 into the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the total energy required to heat the water.

steps.title: Find useful power
steps.content:
The kettle is not perfectly efficient, so only a portion of its total power is used to heat the water. We calculate the **useful power** ($P_{\text{useful}}$) by multiplying the total power rating by the efficiency:

$$P_{\text{useful}} = P_{\text{total}} \times \text{efficiency}$$

Given:
- $P_{\text{total}} = 1500\text{ W}$
- Efficiency = $85.0\% = 0.850$

$$P_{\text{useful}} = 1500 \times 0.850 = 1275\text{ W}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the power rating by the efficiency (as a decimal): 1500 * 0.85

calculator.description: Calculate the useful power output.

steps.title: Calculate operating time
steps.content:
Finally, we use the relationship between power, energy, and time. Power is the rate of energy transfer:

$$P = \frac{E}{t} \implies t = \frac{E}{P}$$

We divide the total energy required ($Q$) by the useful power ($P_{\text{useful}}$):

$$t = \frac{252,000}{1275}$$

$$t \approx 197.65\text{ s}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the energy result from step 1 by the power result from step 2: 252000 / 1275

calculator.description: Divide the energy by the useful power to find the time.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the given values like $0.750\text{ kg}$ and $1500\text{ W}$), we get:

$$t = 198\text{ s}$$

In an exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution of values into the power formula and one mark for the final numerical answer.

steps.title: Identify the energy gap
steps.content: The question states that the kettle is $85.0\%$ efficient. This means that $15.0\%$ of the electrical energy supplied to the kettle is not used for its primary purpose (heating the water). Instead, this energy is "wasted" or dissipated into other parts of the system.

steps.title: Explain thermal dissipation
steps.content:
In a real-world system, thermal energy is naturally transferred to any object in contact with the heat source. For the electric kettle, energy is lost in two main ways:

1.  **To the kettle body:** Thermal energy is conducted from the heating element into the walls and base of the kettle, raising its temperature.
2.  **To the surroundings:** Heat is transferred to the surrounding air through convection (air currents moving past the kettle) and radiation from the kettle's outer surface.

This process is known as **thermal dissipation**.

highlights.text: some energy is dissipated as heat to the kettle body and the surrounding air

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Therefore, not all electrical energy is transferred to the water because some energy is dissipated as heat to the kettle body and the surrounding air.

steps.title: Define efficiency
steps.content:
This question involves **Topic A.3.3 (Power and efficiency)**. Efficiency is the ratio of useful energy output to the total energy input. In this case, the 'useful' energy is the thermal energy transferred specifically to the water to increase its temperature.

$$\text{efficiency} = \frac{\text{useful energy out}}{\text{total energy in}}$$

steps.title: Identify energy dissipation
steps.content:
Not all of the $1500\text{ W}$ of electrical power is used to heat the water. Some of the energy is dissipated to the surroundings. 

To earn the mark, we must recognize that a significant portion of this 'lost' energy is transferred as thermal energy to the kettle body and the surrounding air. However, there are also other energy pathways involved.

highlights.text: State that energy is lost to the kettle or surroundings: some energy is dissipated as heat to the kettle body and the surrounding air

steps.title: Transformation to non-thermal energy
steps.content:
Following the **Transformation to Non-Thermal Energy** approach, we see that some of the electrical energy is converted into forms that are not thermal energy at all:

*   **Sound energy:** The bubbling and boiling process creates vibrations and sound waves.
*   **Light energy:** Energy is used to power indicator lamps or displays on the kettle.

Because some electrical energy is transformed into these non-thermal forms, as well as being dissipated to the surroundings, it cannot all be used to heat the water.

steps.title: Final summary
steps.content: In summary, the efficiency is less than $100\%$ because energy is lost to the kettle body and air, and specifically because a portion of the electrical energy is transformed into non-thermal forms like **sound** and **light**.

steps.title: Identify the phase change
steps.content:
When the water reaches its boiling point and heat continues to be applied, it doesn't get hotter immediately. Instead, the water undergoes a **phase change** from liquid to gas. This process is commonly known as boiling or evaporation.

This concept is a key part of **Topic B.1.2: Phase changes and energy transfer**.

steps.title: Constant temperature during boiling
steps.content:
During this phase change, the thermal energy being supplied is used to break the intermolecular bonds between the water molecules. Because this energy is directed toward changing the state rather than increasing the kinetic energy of the molecules, the temperature of the water **remains constant at $100.0^\circ\text{C}$** until all the liquid has turned into gas.

In your data booklet, this energy transfer is related to the specific latent heat formula:

$$Q = mL$$

steps.title: Identify the conservation principle
steps.content:
Since the system is **thermally insulated**, no thermal energy is exchanged with the surroundings. This means we can apply the principle of conservation of energy. For a system reaching thermal equilibrium, the energy lost by the hotter components must equal the energy gained by the cooler components.

In IB Physics, this is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

steps.title: Compare initial temperatures
steps.content:
To determine which components lose or gain energy, we look at their initial temperatures:
- The copper block is at $350.0\text{ K}$ (the higher temperature).
- The water and the aluminium calorimeter are both at $290.0\text{ K}$ (the lower temperature).

Thermal energy always flows from a higher temperature to a lower temperature. Therefore, the copper block will lose energy, and both the water and the aluminium calorimeter will gain energy.

steps.title: Formulate the energy expression
steps.content:
We can now state the expression for the conservation of energy by equating the heat lost ($Q_{\text{lost}}$) to the heat gained ($Q_{\text{gained}}$):

$$\text{Heat Lost} = \text{Heat Gained}$$

Substituting our specific components into the equation gives us the final expression:

$$Q_{\text{Cu}} = Q_{\text{Al}} + Q_{\text{water}}$$

This accounts for the energy leaving the copper and entering both the calorimeter vessel and the water it contains.

steps.title: Analyze the thermal system
steps.content: This question relates to **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. We have a copper block placed into a calorimeter containing water. The question states the system is **thermally insulated**, which means no heat is exchanged with the surroundings. All heat lost by the hotter object must be gained by the cooler objects.

steps.title: State the conservation principle
steps.content:
In an isolated system, the principle of conservation of energy tells us that the total energy remains constant. Using the **Total Heat Summation** approach, the sum of all heat changes ($Q$) in the system must be zero:

$$\sum Q = 0$$

Alternatively, this is often expressed as:
$$\text{Heat lost} = \text{Heat gained}$$

steps.title: Identify the heat changes
steps.content:
There are three components exchanging heat: the copper block ($Q_{\text{Cu}}$), the aluminium calorimeter ($Q_{\text{Al}}$), and the water ($Q_{\text{water}}$). From the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet, we know each heat change is defined by:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Since the copper starts at a higher temperature ($350.0\text{ K}$) than the water and calorimeter ($290.0\text{ K}$), the copper will lose heat while the others gain it.

steps.title: Write the final expression
steps.content:
Combining these into a single expression for conservation of energy, we get:

$$Q_{\text{Cu}} + Q_{\text{Al}} + Q_{\text{water}} = 0$$

Following the markscheme's logic for "Heat lost = Heat gained", where the magnitude of heat lost by the copper equals the heat gained by the aluminium and water, we can also write:

$$Q_{\text{Cu}} = Q_{\text{Al}} + Q_{\text{water}}$$

Either of these expressions correctly describes the conservation of energy in this system.

steps.title: State the conservation principle
steps.content:
Since the system is described as being **thermally insulated**, no heat enters or leaves the system from the outside environment. Therefore, we apply the principle of **conservation of energy**, which states that the total heat energy lost by the hot substance must be equal to the total heat energy gained by the cold substances.

$$Q_{\text{lost}} = Q_{\text{gained}}$$

In this scenario, the copper block (at $350.0\text{ K}$) loses energy, while the calorimeter and water (at $290.0\text{ K}$) gain it.

steps.title: Identify the energy formula
steps.content:
To express these heat transfers mathematically, we use the specific heat capacity formula found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your IB Data Booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Where:
- $m$ is the mass in $\text{kg}$
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity
- $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature

steps.title: Construct the expanded expression
steps.content:
Using the **Expanded Variable Expression** approach, we substitute the $mc\Delta T$ formula for each of the three components in the system (copper, aluminium, and water) to show their relationship:

$$m_{\text{Cu}}c_{\text{Cu}}\Delta T_{\text{Cu}} = m_{\text{Al}}c_{\text{Al}}\Delta T_{\text{Al}} + m_{\text{water}}c_{\text{water}}\Delta T_{\text{water}}$$

If we define $\Delta T$ as the positive difference in temperature for each component, where $T_{\text{final}}$ is the equilibrium temperature:

$$m_{\text{Cu}}c_{\text{Cu}}(350.0 - T_{\text{final}}) = m_{\text{Al}}c_{\text{Al}}(T_{\text{final}} - 290.0) + m_{\text{water}}c_{\text{water}}(T_{\text{final}} - 290.0)$$

This expression correctly accounts for the energy balance of all three substances.

 - Correct expression with units and symbols identified <marks>A1</marks>

To calculate the energy transferred from the copper block, we use the specific heat capacity formula found in **Topic B.1 Thermal energy transfers** of the syllabus.

steps.title: Identify copper block properties

From the question, we identify the specific mass and heat capacity for the copper block:

- Mass of copper, $m = 3.00\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Define the temperature change
steps.content:
The copper block starts at $350.0\ \text{K}$ and cools down to a final temperature, $T_{\text{final}}$. The change in temperature (or temperature drop) is expressed as:

$$\Delta T = (350.0 - T_{\text{final}})$$

Using $(T_{\text{initial}} - T_{\text{final}})$ ensures that the value for "heat lost" is expressed as a positive magnitude.

steps.title: Substitute into the formula
steps.content:
By substituting these values into the heat equation, we obtain the final expression for the total heat lost by the copper block, $Q_{\text{Cu}}$:

$$Q_{\text{Cu}} = 3.00 \times 385 \times (350.0 - T_{\text{final}})$$ 

This expression correctly relates the given constants and the unknown final state required by the markscheme.

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the heat gained, we use the specific heat capacity equation from the **Thermal Physics** section of the data booklet (covered in **Topic B.1**).

$$Q = mc
\Delta T$$

Where:
*   $m$ is the mass in kg
*   $c$ is the specific heat capacity in $\text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$
*   $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature

steps.title: Determine temperature difference
steps.content:
Both the aluminium calorimeter and the water start at an initial temperature of $290.0\ \text{K}$ and heat up to the final equilibrium temperature $T_{\text{final}}$.

The temperature difference ($\Delta T$) for the heat gained is:

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - 290.0$$

steps.title: Expression for aluminium
steps.content:
For the aluminium calorimeter, substitute the given mass ($1.50\ \text{kg}$) and its specific heat capacity ($900\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{Al}} &= m_{\text{Al}} c_{\text{Al}} \Delta T \\ Q_{\text{Al}} &= 1.50 \times 900 \times (T_{\text{final}} - 290.0) \end{aligned}$$

Calculating the coefficient:
$$Q_{\text{Al}} = 1350(T_{\text{final}} - 290.0)$$

steps.title: Expression for water
steps.content:
For the water, substitute the given mass ($2.00\ \text{kg}$) and its specific heat capacity ($4200\ \text{J kg}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{water}} &= m_{\text{water}} c_{\text{water}} \Delta T \\ Q_{\text{water}} &= 2.00 \times 4200 \times (T_{\text{final}} - 290.0) \end{aligned}$$

Calculating the coefficient:
$$Q_{\text{water}} = 8400(T_{\text{final}} - 290.0)$$
