parts.content: Determine the final equilibrium temperature of the system.
parts.markscheme:
- Equate heat lost to heat gained:
   $3.00 \times 385 \times (350.0 - T) = 1.50 \times 900 \times (T - 290.0) + 2.00 \times 4200 \times (T - 290.0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
 - Attempt to rearrange and solve:
   $1155(350.0 - T) = 1350(T - 290.0) + 8400(T - 290.0)$
   $404250 - 1155T = 9750T - 2827500$
   $10905T = 3231750$ <marks>M1</marks>
 - Correct final answer:
   $T = 296.4\ \text{K}$ (Accept 296 K) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the conservation of energy
steps.content:
Since the system is thermally insulated, we apply the principle of conservation of energy. The thermal energy lost by the hot copper block must equal the total thermal energy gained by the cooler water and aluminium calorimeter.

$$\text{Heat Lost by Copper} = \text{Heat Gained by Al} + \text{Heat Gained by Water}$$

steps.title: Select the correct formula
steps.content:
For temperature changes without phase changes, we use the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Where:
* $m$ is mass (kg)
* $c$ is specific heat capacity (J kg$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$)
* $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature (K)

steps.title: Set up heat lost expression
steps.content:
First, calculate the energy lost by the copper block. Since copper starts at a higher temperature ($350.0$ K), its temperature will decrease to the final equilibrium temperature $T$. We use a positive temperature difference $(350.0 - T)$.

Using $m_{\text{Cu}} = 3.00$ kg and $c_{\text{Cu}} = 385$ J kg$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{lost}} &= 3.00 \times 385 \times (350.0 - T) \\ &= 1155(350.0 - T) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up heat gained expression
steps.content:
Next, calculate the energy gained by the aluminium and water. Both start at $290.0$ K and warm up to $T$. The positive temperature difference is $(T - 290.0)$.

For aluminium ($m=1.50, c=900$):
$$Q_{\text{Al}} = 1.50 \times 900 \times (T - 290.0) = 1350(T - 290.0)$$

For water ($m=2.00, c=4200$):
$$Q_{\text{water}} = 2.00 \times 4200 \times (T - 290.0) = 8400(T - 290.0)$$

steps.title: Form the main equation
steps.content:
Combine the expressions into our conservation equation ($Q_{\text{lost}} = Q_{\text{gained}}$). This step earns the first method mark.

$$1155(350.0 - T) = 1350(T - 290.0) + 8400(T - 290.0)$$

Grouping the terms on the right side:

$$1155(350.0 - T) = (1350 + 8400)(T - 290.0)$$
$$1155(350.0 - T) = 9750(T - 290.0)$$

steps.title: Use calculator to solve
steps.content: You can solve this linear equation directly using your calculator's equation solver or graphing features. This avoids algebraic errors.

gdc.instructions: Use the nSolve or Equation Solver app. Enter 1155(350-x) = 9750(x-290) and solve.

ti84.instructions: Press [Math] > [Solver]. Enter the equation 1155(350-X) - 9750(X-290) = 0 and solve for X.

desmos.instructions: Graph the equations y = 1155(350-x) and y = 9750(x-290). Find the x-coordinate of the intersection point.

calculator.description: Solve for T by finding the intersection of Heat Lost and Heat Gained

steps.title: Solve algebraically
steps.content:
To solve manually, expand the brackets:

$$\begin{aligned} 404250 - 1155T &= 9750T - 2827500 \\ 404250 + 2827500 &= 9750T + 1155T \\ 3231750 &= 10905T \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final temperature
steps.content:
Divide to isolate $T$:

$$T = \frac{3231750}{10905} \approx 296.35\ \text{K}$$

Rounding to 4 significant figures (consistent with the data):

$$T = 296.4\ \text{K}$$

steps.title: Apply conservation of energy
steps.content:
Since the system is thermally insulated, we apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics**. The principle of conservation of energy states that the algebraic sum of all energy changes in an isolated system is zero.

We set the sum of all heat transfers $Q$ to zero:

$$\Sigma Q = 0$$

$$Q_{\text{copper}} + Q_{\text{calorimeter}} + Q_{\text{water}} = 0$$

steps.title: Select formula for heat transfer
steps.content:
For each component, we calculate the heat transfer using the specific heat capacity equation from the data booklet. We must define the change in temperature strictly as $\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$.

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Let $T_f$ be the final equilibrium temperature.

steps.title: Define expressions for each component
steps.content:
We substitute the given mass ($m$), specific heat capacity ($c$), and initial temperature ($T_{\text{initial}}$) for each part of the system.

**Copper Block** (starts at $350.0\ \text{K}$):
$$Q_{\text{Cu}} = 3.00 \times 385 \times (T_f - 350.0)$$

**Aluminium Calorimeter** (starts at $290.0\ \text{K}$):
$$Q_{\text{Al}} = 1.50 \times 900 \times (T_f - 290.0)$$

**Water** (starts at $290.0\ \text{K}$):
$$Q_{\text{water}} = 2.00 \times 4200 \times (T_f - 290.0)$$

steps.title: Set up the main equation
steps.content:
Substitute these expressions into our conservation equation $\Sigma Q = 0$. Note that calculating the coefficients ($mc$) simplifies the algebra.

**Calculate coefficients:**
- Copper: $3.00 \times 385 = 1155$
- Aluminium: $1.50 \times 900 = 1350$
- Water: $2.00 \times 4200 = 8400$

**Equation:**
$$1155(T_f - 350.0) + 1350(T_f - 290.0) + 8400(T_f - 290.0) = 0$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of mass and specific heat for each substance.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the product of mass and specific heat for each substance.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the product of mass and specific heat for each substance.

calculator.description: Calculate mc for each component

steps.title: Group terms and solve
steps.content:
Since the water and calorimeter share the same initial temperature and variable terms, we can group them together.

$$1155(T_f - 350.0) + (1350 + 8400)(T_f - 290.0) = 0$$
$$1155(T_f - 350.0) + 9750(T_f - 290.0) = 0$$

Expand the brackets:
$$1155T_f - 404250 + 9750T_f - 2827500 = 0$$

Combine $T_f$ terms and constants:
$$(1155 + 9750)T_f = 404250 + 2827500$$
$$10905T_f = 3231750$$

gdc.instructions: Use the Equation Solver application. Enter the equation 1155(x-350) + 1350(x-290) + 8400(x-290) = 0 and solve.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > Numeric Solver. Enter eqn: 1155(X-350) + 1350(X-290) + 8400(X-290) = 0. Solve for X.

desmos.instructions: Type the equation 1155(x-350) + 1350(x-290) + 8400(x-290) = 0. Click the vertical line intersection to find the x-value.

calculator.description: Solve for Tf using a linear equation solver or graphing

steps.title: Calculate final answer
steps.content:
Divide to find $T_f$:

$$T_f = \frac{3231750}{10905}$$

$$T_f \approx 296.355\ \text{K}$$

Rounding to one decimal place as per the data provided (290.0, 350.0):

$$T_{\text{final}} = 296.4\ \text{K}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the total energy constant by the total heat capacity.

ti84.instructions: Divide the total energy constant by the total heat capacity.

desmos.instructions: Divide the total energy constant by the total heat capacity.

calculator.description: Perform final division

steps.title: Relation to markscheme
steps.content:
The markscheme uses the method "Heat Lost = Heat Gained". Notice that our equation $\Sigma Q = 0$ is mathematically equivalent:

If we move the copper term (which has negative $Q$ because it cools down) to the other side, we get:
$$1350(T_f - 290) + 8400(T_f - 290) = 1155(350 - T_f)$$

This matches the markscheme's first line: $1.50 \times 900 \times (T - 290.0) + 2.00 \times 4200 \times (T - 290.0) = 3.00 \times 385 \times (350.0 - T)$.

Both methods are valid and yield full marks.

steps.title: Define the method
steps.content:
To solve this problem efficiently, we will use the **Combined Heat Capacity** approach. Instead of treating the aluminium calorimeter and the water as separate terms in the final equation, we first calculate the total heat capacity ($C$) of the entire "cold reservoir" (calorimeter + water).

The formula for heat capacity is derived from the thermal energy equation:
$$C = mc$$

steps.title: Calculate cold reservoir capacity

calculator.description: Calculate the total heat capacity of the cold reservoir

steps.title: Set up energy balance
steps.content:
The copper block loses heat while the cold reservoir gains heat until they reach equilibrium temperature $T$. We equate the heat lost to the heat gained.

Heat lost by Copper = Heat gained by Cold Reservoir
$$m_{\text{Cu}} c_{\text{Cu}} (T_{\text{initial, Cu}} - T) = C_{\text{cold}} (T - T_{\text{initial, cold}})$$

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Substitute the known values into the equation. For the copper side, we first calculate $m_{\text{Cu}}c_{\text{Cu}} = 3.00 \times 385 = 1155\ \text{J K}^{-1}$.

$$1155(350.0 - T) = 9750(T - 290.0)$$

calculator.description: Calculate heat capacity of the copper block

steps.title: Solve for temperature
steps.content:
Now, solve for $T$ by expanding and rearranging the terms.

$$\begin{aligned} 404250 - 1155T &= 9750T - 2827500 \\ 404250 + 2827500 &= 9750T + 1155T \\ 3231750 &= 10905T \\ T &= \frac{3231750}{10905} \\ T &\approx 296.35\ \text{K} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Solve for T

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The calculated value is $296.35\dots\ \text{K}$. 

We must consider significant figures. The masses ($3.00$, $1.50$, $2.00$) are given to **3 significant figures**, while the temperatures are given to 4. In multiplication and division, the result is limited by the least precise data, so we round to 3 significant figures.

$$T = 296\ \text{K}$$ 

(Note: The markscheme also accepts $296.4\ \text{K}$, which corresponds to 4 significant figures).

parts.content: Explain one reason why the actual final temperature might differ from the calculated value.
parts.markscheme:
- Heat losses to the environment or calorimeter not being perfectly insulated. <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the theoretical assumption
steps.content: In the calculation, we assumed the system was perfectly **thermally insulated**. This implies that the Law of Conservation of Energy applies strictly within the system, with zero energy transfer to or from the outside world ($$Q_{\text{lost}} = Q_{\text{gained}}$$).

steps.title: Acknowledge real-world limitations
steps.content: However, in a practical experiment, a perfectly insulated system is impossible to achieve. No matter how good the insulation is, the calorimeter cannot completely block heat flow.

steps.title: Describe heat loss
steps.content: Since the final mixture is likely warmer than the room temperature, thermal energy will flow from the system to the cooler environment. This is known as **heat loss to the surroundings**.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Because some thermal energy escapes the system instead of heating the water and calorimeter, the actual final temperature measured would be lower than the theoretical value calculated.

options.markscheme: This expression omits the factor of 4 resulting from the $4\pi$ in the surface area of the sphere (intensity formula) and the lack of $\pi$ in the cross-sectional area calculation.
This expression calculates the power reflected by the planet rather than the power absorbed, as it uses $\alpha$ instead of $(1 - \alpha)$.

- The intensity of radiation at distance $d$ is $I = \frac{L}{4 \pi d^{2}}$.
- The power incident on the planet is $P_{\text{incident}} = I \times \text{cross-sectional area} = I \times \pi R^{2}$.
- Substituting $I$ gives $P_{\text{incident}} = \frac{L \pi R^{2}}{4 \pi d^{2}} = \frac{L R^{2}}{4 d^{2}}$.
- The power absorbed is $P_{\text{absorbed}} = P_{\text{incident}} \times (1 - \alpha) = \frac{L R^{2} (1 - \alpha)}{4 d^{2}}$.
This expression incorrectly retains a factor of $\pi$ in the denominator, failing to cancel it with the $\pi$ from the planet's cross-sectional area.

Two metal blocks, X and Y, are supplied with the same quantity of thermal energy. The mass and the increase in temperature for each block are shown in the table.

| Block | Mass | Temperature increase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| X | $$2m$$ | $$4\Delta T$$ |
| Y | $$m$$ | $$3\Delta T$$ |

Which of the following is the ratio $\frac{\text{specific heat capacity of X}}{\text{specific heat capacity of Y}}$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.1 Thermal energy transfers > B.1.1 Understanding thermal energy transfer mechanisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1 Understanding thermal energy transfer mechanisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the relationship for thermal energy transfer to a substance, Q = mcΔT, to compare specific heat capacities given equal energy input, different masses, and temperature rises.

options.markscheme: This ratio results from incorrectly assuming that specific heat capacity is proportional to the temperature increase divided by the mass ($c \propto \frac{\Delta T}{m}$).
This ratio results from incorrectly assuming that specific heat capacity is proportional to the mass divided by the temperature increase ($c \propto \frac{m}{\Delta T}$).

The thermal energy supplied $$Q$$ is the same for both blocks ($$Q_X = Q_Y$$).
Using the formula for thermal energy transfer $$Q = mc\Delta T$$, the specific heat capacity $$c$$ is given by $$c = \frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$$.
For block X: $$c_X = \frac{Q}{(2m)(4\Delta T)} = \frac{Q}{8m\Delta T}$$.
For block Y: $$c_Y = \frac{Q}{(m)(3\Delta T)} = \frac{Q}{3m\Delta T}$$.
The ratio of the specific heat capacities is:
$$\frac{c_X}{c_Y} = \frac{\frac{Q}{8m\Delta T}}{\frac{Q}{3m\Delta T}} = \frac{Q}{8m\Delta T} \times \frac{3m\Delta T}{Q} = \frac{3}{8}$$.
This is the inverse ratio, representing $$\frac{c_Y}{c_X}$$ instead of $$\frac{c_X}{c_Y}$$.

parts.content: Describe the relationship between mass and temperature change.
parts.markscheme:
Describe relationship
- As mass increases, temperature change decreases.

steps.title: Identify the data columns
steps.content: To describe the relationship, we first need to look at how the variables are arranged in the provided table. We have **Mass** ($m$) in the left column and **Temperature change** ($\Delta T$) in the right column.

steps.title: Observe the mass trend
steps.content:
Looking down the first column, we can see that the values for mass are steadily increasing:

$$0.050 \rightarrow 0.060 \rightarrow 0.075 \rightarrow 0.090 \rightarrow 0.100$$

steps.title: Observe the temperature trend
steps.content:
Now, let's look at what happens to the temperature change in the second column as the mass increases. The values are steadily decreasing:

$$24.0 \rightarrow 20.0 \rightarrow 16.0 \rightarrow 13.3 \rightarrow 12.0$$

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
By comparing these two observations, we can conclude that as one variable goes up, the other goes down. This is the definition of an inverse relationship (though for a 1-mark 'describe' question, we just need to state the direction of the change).

**As mass increases, temperature change decreases.**

highlights.text: Describe the relationship between mass and temperature change.

steps.title: Observe the data trend
steps.content: To describe the relationship, let's first look at the values in the table. Notice that as the mass $m$ increases from $0.050\text{ kg}$ to $0.100\text{ kg}$, the temperature change $\Delta T$ consistently decreases from $24.0\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $12.0\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$. This tells us they have an **inverse** relationship.

highlights.text: Describe the relationship between mass and temperature change.

steps.title: Perform proportionality check
steps.content:
We can confirm the nature of this relationship mathematically. If two variables are inversely proportional, their product should be a constant ($m \times \Delta T = k$). Let's test this using a few rows from the table:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Trial 1: } 0.050 \times 24.0 &= 1.20 \\ \text{Trial 2: } 0.060 \times 20.0 &= 1.20 \\ \text{Trial 5: } 0.100 \times 12.0 &= 1.20 \end{aligned}$$

Since the product is constant, the relationship is exactly inversely proportional.

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To understand how mass ($m$) and temperature change ($\Delta T$) are related, we look at the formula for thermal energy transfer in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**.

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q$ is the thermal energy supplied
- $m$ is the mass of the sample
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity of the material
- $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content:
The question specifies that the "same amount of thermal energy is supplied in each trial" ($Q$ is constant) and the student uses "identical blocks of copper" (specific heat capacity $c$ is constant). 

Because $Q$ and $c$ are constant, the formula shows that the product of mass and temperature change must also be constant:

$$m \Delta T = \frac{Q}{c} = \text{constant}$$

highlights.text: same amount of thermal energy is supplied

steps.title: Analyze the inverse relationship
steps.content:
If the product $m \Delta T$ is a constant value, then $m$ and $\Delta T$ have an **inverse relationship**. We can rearrange the formula to see this more clearly:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\text{constant}}{m}$$

This mathematical relationship tells us that as the denominator (mass) gets larger, the result (temperature change) must get smaller.

parts.content: Explain, using energy principles, why smaller masses show greater temperature increases.
parts.markscheme:
Energy explanation
- Thermal energy is distributed over more mass.
- Larger masses require more energy for the same temperature rise (or smaller masses require less energy for the same rise).

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To explain the relationship between mass and temperature change, we use the specific heat capacity formula from the **Thermal Physics** section of the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q$ is the thermal energy supplied.
- $m$ is the mass of the sample.
- $c$ is the specific heat capacity of copper.
- $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature.

steps.title: Rearrange for temperature change
steps.content:
We want to see how the temperature change $\Delta T$ depends on the mass $m$. We can rearrange the formula to isolate $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{mc}$$

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content:
In this experiment, the question specifies that the "same amount of thermal energy is supplied in each trial." Additionally, since the material is always copper, the specific heat capacity ($c$) remains constant. 

Since $Q$ and $c$ are constant, we can see that:

$$\Delta T \propto \frac{1}{m}$$

This shows that the temperature change is **inversely proportional** to the mass.

highlights.text: same amount of thermal energy is supplied in each trial

steps.title: Explain using energy principles
steps.content:
Based on the algebraic relationship $\Delta T = \frac{Q}{mc}$:
1. For a **smaller mass**, the fixed amount of thermal energy ($Q$) is distributed over fewer atoms/particles. 
2. This results in a higher energy gain per unit mass, which manifests as a greater increase in temperature. 

Conversely, a larger mass requires more energy to achieve the same temperature rise because the energy must be spread across more material.

steps.title: Identify constant energy supply
steps.content: The question states that the **same amount of thermal energy** is supplied to each copper block. This means the total energy available to increase the internal energy of the sample is constant across all trials.

highlights.text: same amount of thermal energy is supplied

steps.title: Analyze energy distribution
steps.content:
Think of the thermal energy as being "shared" among all the particles in the block. A smaller mass contains fewer particles compared to a larger mass. 

Because the total energy $Q$ is the same, in a smaller mass, this energy is distributed over fewer particles. This means each individual particle (or each unit of mass) receives a **larger share** of the total energy.

steps.title: Link energy to temperature
steps.content:
Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles. Since each particle in the smaller mass gains more energy on average, the increase in average kinetic energy is greater. 

Consequently, the smaller mass shows a **greater temperature increase** ($\Delta T$). As noted in the markscheme, for larger masses, the same thermal energy is distributed over more mass, resulting in a smaller rise per unit.

highlights.text: Thermal energy is distributed over more mass.

parts.content: The student plots a graph of $\Delta T$ against $\frac{1}{m}$. Estimate the specific heat capacity of copper, if energy supplied to each sample is $Q=480 \text{ J}$.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate $1 / m$
- Correct values to 3 significant figures: $20.0, 16.7, 13.3, 11.1, 10.0$

Use graph to estimate $c$
- Gradient from graph: $\Delta T /(1 / m) \approx 1.20 \, { }^{\circ}\text{C} \text{ kg}$.
- From $\Delta T=\frac{Q}{m c} \Rightarrow$ gradient $=\frac{Q}{c}$.
- Rearranged: $c=\frac{Q}{\text { gradient }}=\frac{480}{1.20}=4.0 \times 10^{2} \text{ J} /\left(\text{kg } { }^{\circ} \text{C}\right)$ (accept values around 400).

parts.content: Suggest one assumption made in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Experimental assumption
- Assumes no heat is lost to surroundings / thermal energy is transferred to copper only.

steps.title: Analyze the energy transfer
steps.content:
In this experiment, we are investigating the relationship between mass ($m$) and temperature change ($\Delta T$) when a fixed amount of thermal energy ($Q$) is supplied. 

This is described by the specific heat capacity formula found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

To make this equation work perfectly for our data, we have to assume that every single Joule of energy produced by the heater actually ends up inside the copper block.

highlights.text: Each block is heated for the same time using an electric heater of fixed power.

steps.title: Identify potential energy losses
steps.content:
In a real laboratory setting, energy doesn't always go exactly where we want it. Some thermal energy might:
1. Escape into the surrounding air via convection or radiation.
2. Be absorbed by the surface the block is sitting on.
3. Remain in the heater itself after it is turned off.

For the student's results to follow the theoretical model accurately, we must assume these losses are negligible.

steps.title: State the assumption
steps.content:
Using the **Thermal Insulation Assumption**, we conclude that the student assumes the system is perfectly insulated.

The most common way to state this for marks is that **no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings**, or that **all thermal energy from the heater is transferred to the copper block only**.

highlights.text: Assumes no heat is lost to surroundings / thermal energy is transferred to copper only.

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content:
In this experiment, the student uses an electric heater to provide thermal energy to various copper blocks. According to **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**, the relationship between the energy supplied and the resulting temperature change is given by the specific heat capacity formula:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

To analyze the data correctly, we must assume that the energy we calculate (from the heater's power and time) is exactly equal to the energy absorbed by the copper.

steps.title: Consider energy losses
steps.content:
In a real-world laboratory setting, not all thermal energy from the heater stays within the copper block. Some energy is inevitably lost to the surrounding air through convection or to the surface the block is resting on through conduction. 

For the equation $$Q = mc\Delta T$$ to be applied simply, we must assume that **no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings**.

highlights.text: Assumes no heat is lost to surroundings / thermal energy is transferred to copper only.

steps.title: Assume constant heat capacity
steps.content:
Using the **Constant Specific Heat Capacity** approach, we also make a fundamental assumption about the material itself. We assume that the specific heat capacity ($c$) of the copper is a constant value and does not change as the temperature of the block increases during the experiment.

Without this assumption, the relationship between $m$ and $\Delta T$ would be non-linear and much harder to analyze.

steps.title: Finalize the suggestion
steps.content: The most common assumption expected in IB thermal physics experiments is the neglect of external heat exchange. Therefore, a valid suggestion is that **all thermal energy from the heater is transferred to the copper block only**, with no losses to the environment.

steps.title: Identify the physics relationship
steps.content:
In this experiment, we are looking at the relationship between mass ($m$) and temperature change ($\Delta T$) when a fixed amount of energy ($Q$) is supplied. This falls under **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. The core formula we use is found in the Thermal Physics section of your data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

To make sense of the data, we must assume that certain factors remain constant or behave ideally.

steps.title: Assume no heat loss
steps.content:
The most common assumption in thermal experiments is that **no thermal energy is lost to the surroundings**. 

If heat were lost to the air or the surface the block is sitting on, the energy ($Q$) actually absorbed by the copper would be less than the energy supplied by the heater. For the experiment to be fair across different masses, we assume that 100% of the energy from the heater is transferred to the copper block only.

highlights.text: the same amount of thermal energy is supplied in each trial

steps.title: Uniform temperature distribution
steps.content:
Using the **Uniform Temperature Distribution** approach, we also assume that the thermal energy spreads instantly and evenly throughout the block. 

In a real-world scenario, the copper near the heater might be hotter than the copper at the edges. By assuming a uniform distribution, we ensure that the temperature measured by the probe at a single point accurately represents the $\Delta T$ of the entire mass ($m$).

steps.title: State the final assumption
steps.content: According to the IB markscheme, the primary answer expected is that **no heat is lost to the surroundings**. This ensures that the energy $Q$ remains a controlled variable throughout all trials.

parts.content: Explain how this assumption could affect the calculated value of the specific heat capacity.
parts.markscheme:
Explanation
- If heat escapes, measured $\Delta T$ is too small (for a given mass).
- This leads to a smaller gradient, and therefore the calculated $c$ is too high ($c = Q/\text{gradient}$).

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To understand how the calculated specific heat capacity ($c$) is affected, we start with the fundamental equation for thermal energy from the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

To see how $c$ is calculated, we rearrange this formula to solve for $c$:

$$c = \frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$$

steps.title: Analyze the impact of heat loss
steps.content:
The assumption usually made in these experiments is that all the thermal energy ($Q$) supplied by the heater is absorbed by the copper block. 

However, if heat is lost to the surroundings, less energy is actually used to increase the temperature of the copper. This means that for any given mass ($m$) and energy supplied ($Q$), the measured temperature change ($\Delta T$) will be **smaller** than the theoretical value.

steps.title: Evaluate the algebraic effect
steps.content:
Now, let's look at our rearranged formula again:

$$c = \frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$$

In this relationship, the specific heat capacity $c$ is **inversely proportional** to the temperature change $\Delta T$. 

If the measured $\Delta T$ is smaller than it should be (because of heat loss), it appears in the denominator of our calculation. Dividing by a smaller number results in a larger final value.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: Because the measured $\Delta T$ is too low due to heat escaping, the calculated value of the specific heat capacity $c$ will be **too high** (overestimated).

steps.title: Establish the Linear Relationship
steps.content:
To see how a graph helps us find the specific heat capacity ($c$), we start with the thermal energy equation from the data booklet:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

In this experiment, the student varies mass ($m$) and measures temperature change ($\Delta T$) while keeping energy ($Q$) constant. We can rearrange this to show $\Delta T$ as a function of $1/m$:

$$\Delta T = \left( \frac{Q}{c} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{m}$$

If we plot $\Delta T$ on the $y$-axis and $1/m$ on the $x$-axis, the graph is a straight line through the origin where the **gradient ($k$)** is equal to $\frac{Q}{c}$.

steps.title: Analyze Heat Loss Impact
steps.content:
The assumption is that all thermal energy from the heater enters the copper block. However, in a real experiment, some heat is lost to the surroundings. 

Because of this heat loss, the actual energy absorbed by the copper is less than the electrical energy supplied. This means the measured temperature change ($\Delta T$) for any given mass will be **lower** than it would be in a perfect system.

steps.title: Effect on the Gradient
steps.content:
Let's look at how lower $\Delta T$ values affect our graph of $\Delta T$ vs $1/m$:

1. The $x$-values ($1/m$) remain the same.
2. The $y$-values ($\Delta T$) are all shifted downwards.

This results in a line of best fit that is less steep. Therefore, the **gradient ($k$) of the graph decreases** due to the heat loss.

steps.title: Determine Effect on $c$
steps.content:
Finally, we relate the gradient back to the specific heat capacity. From our initial equation, we know:

$$\text{gradient } (k) = \frac{Q}{c} \implies c = \frac{Q}{k}$$

Since $c$ is inversely proportional to the gradient, a **smaller gradient** leads to a **larger calculated value for $c$**. 

Consequently, the assumption of no heat loss causes the specific heat capacity to be **overestimated** (the calculated value is higher than the true value).

questions.specification:
Four different solid samples are heated using independent electric heaters. The table below lists the mass of each sample, the total thermal energy transferred to it, and the resulting change in its temperature.

| Material | Mass ($kg$) | Thermal energy transferred ($J$) | Temperature change ($K$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P | $0.40$ | $1200$ | $15$ |
| Q | $0.60$ | $1800$ | $10$ |
| R | $0.50$ | $2500$ | $10$ |
| S | $0.80$ | $1600$ | $10$ |

Which material possesses the greatest specific heat capacity?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.1 Thermal energy transfers > B.1.1 Understanding thermal energy transfer mechanisms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.1.1 Understanding thermal energy transfer mechanisms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which material has the greatest specific heat capacity using given mass, thermal energy transferred, and temperature change (via Q = mcΔT), which is a core calculation/application of thermal energy transfer.

options.markscheme: Using $c=\frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$: $c_P=\frac{1200}{0.40\times 15}=\frac{1200}{6}=200\ \text{J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, not the greatest.
Using $c=\frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$: $c_Q=\frac{1800}{0.60\times 10}=\frac{1800}{6}=300\ \text{J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, not the greatest.
Using $c=\frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$: $c_R=\frac{2500}{0.50\times 10}=\frac{2500}{5}=500\ \text{J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, which is the greatest.
Using $c=\frac{Q}{m\Delta T}$: $c_S=\frac{1600}{0.80\times 10}=\frac{1600}{8}=200\ \text{J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$, not the greatest.

options.content: Thermal radiation requires a medium to transfer heat.
Only solids can emit thermal radiation.
Thermal radiation can transfer heat through a vacuum.
Radiation is a type of heat transfer that only occurs at high temperatures.
